cases.name: int and double render differently when inlined
cases.note: The single most important case in the corpus, and the reason input values carry a type tag. `integral_double` is the trap: a double whose value happens to be integral (5.0). JavaScript cannot represent the difference from int 5 at all, so it emits `@p0 tinyint` and `= 5` for BOTH — and that is the frozen contract. Dart CAN represent the difference, and by default would emit `@p0 float` and `= 5.0` for the double. Worse, Dart disagrees with itself: `5.0 is int` is false on the Dart VM but true on dart2js, and `(5.0).toString()` is '5.0' on the VM but '5' on the web. So a naive Dart port fails this case on Flutter mobile and passes it on Flutter web. Dart must normalize an integral double to its integer rendering. Only `d` (5.5, genuinely fractional) may render as a float.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[i] = @p0 AND [m].[integral_double] = @p1 AND [m].[d] = @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 float', @p0 = 5, @p1 = 5, @p2 = 5.5;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[i] = 5 AND [m].[integral_double] = 5 AND [m].[d] = 5.5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`i` = ? AND `m`.`integral_double` = ? AND `m`.`d` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`i` = 5 AND `m`.`integral_double` = 5 AND `m`.`d` = 5.5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = $1 AND "m"."integral_double" = $2 AND "m"."d" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = 5 AND "m"."integral_double" = 5 AND "m"."d" = 5.5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = ? AND "m"."integral_double" = ? AND "m"."d" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."i" = 5 AND "m"."integral_double" = 5 AND "m"."d" = 5.5;

cases.name: a literal ? or $ in a raw fragment is not a placeholder
cases.note: Regression: the placeholder walk scanned the rendered SQL for a bare ?/$ and could not tell it from caller text, corrupting the literal and misaligning the bound params.

prepared.sql: SELECT 'why?' AS q, '$100' AS price FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = $1;

postgres.raw: SELECT 'why?' AS q, '$100' AS price FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 42;

prepared.sql: SELECT 'why?' AS q, '$100' AS price FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT 'why?' AS q, '$100' AS price FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 42;

cases.name: select column without an alias
cases.note: "No alias" is modelled here as an ABSENT `alias` field, and the TS driver maps absent to the empty string. The emitter suppresses ` AS ...` only on a strict `alias !== ''` test. A Dart port that models the alias as a nullable `String?` and forgets to fold null into '' will emit `AS []` / `AS ""` (an empty quoted identifier) instead of omitting the alias entirely. Every dialect breaks.

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`name` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` = 1;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 1;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 1;

cases.name: select all with a where on a bound boolean
cases.note: The seed case 'select all from table' plus one WHERE, and the pair now pins that a WHERE changes nothing except the WHERE. Until 7.0.0 it changed a great deal: MSSQL injected a safety-net `TOP (1000)` on an unbounded outer SELECT, and any WHERE state suppressed it — so this op-list and the seed case had genuinely different MSSQL shapes. 7.0.0 removed the cap, so all four dialects now differ from the seed case only by the appended predicate. What is left under test is the bound boolean: MSSQL declares `@p0 bit` and passes `@p0 = 1`, not `true`, while the other three bind their own placeholder shape (`?`, `$1`, `?`) and carry the value out in `params`. Raw mode inlines the literal `true` on all four.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ?;

cases.name: select distinct after the columns is order independent
cases.note: `distinct()` sets a flag on the query state, it does not push a select state — so calling it AFTER the columns must render identically to calling it before them (see 'select distinct with two columns'). A port that appends DISTINCT as a positional token in the select list emits `SELECT "u"."name", DISTINCT ...`.

cases.name: select distinct with select all
cases.note: `SELECT DISTINCT *`, identical on all four dialects. Before 7.0.0 this case carried the DISTINCT/TOP token-order contract: MSSQL's automatic cap made the golden `SELECT DISTINCT TOP (1000) *`, which proved the TOP hook runs after the DISTINCT keyword and before the column list. The cap is gone, so that ordering is now observable only through an explicit `.top(n)` — see the next case, where MSSQL emits `SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) *`. Reversing the two (`SELECT TOP (10) DISTINCT *`) is still a T-SQL syntax error; this case simply no longer demonstrates it.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT DISTINCT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];

prepared.sql: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM `users` AS `u`;

mysql.raw: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM `users` AS `u`;

prepared.sql: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

postgres.raw: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

prepared.sql: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM "users" AS "u";

sqlite.raw: SELECT DISTINCT * FROM "users" AS "u";

cases.name: select distinct with an explicit top
cases.note: `.top(n)` renders through the `beforeSelectColumns` hook, which defaultSelect calls after it has written `SELECT ` and the `DISTINCT ` keyword and before the column list — hence `SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) *`. Emitting `SELECT TOP (10) DISTINCT *` instead is a T-SQL syntax error. MSSQL only: the case declares no other dialect, because top is a silent no-op everywhere else (see 'top is silently ignored by the non-mssql dialects'). Since 7.0.0 removed the automatic cap, an explicit top is the only thing that can put a TOP in a SELECT list at all.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT DISTINCT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];

cases.name: select scalar subquery whose value precedes the where value
cases.note: PARAM ORDERING. The subquery in the SELECT list binds 'shipped' and the outer WHERE binds true. Clauses are rendered SELECT-then-FROM-then-WHERE, so the subquery's value must be param 0 and the WHERE's value param 1 — Postgres numbers them $1/$2 in that order, and MSSQL declares `@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 bit`. A port that collects bound values per-clause and concatenates WHERE values before SELECT values produces syntactically valid SQL with the arguments silently transposed: the worst possible failure, because nothing throws.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[name] AS [userName], (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[status] = @p0) AS [shippedCount] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 bit', @p0 = N'shipped', @p1 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT [u].[name] AS [userName], (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[status] = shipped) AS [shippedCount] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`name` AS `userName`, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`status` = ?) AS `shippedCount` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`name` AS `userName`, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`status` = shipped) AS `shippedCount` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."name" AS "userName", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = $1) AS "shippedCount" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."name" AS "userName", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = shipped) AS "shippedCount" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."name" AS "userName", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = ?) AS "shippedCount" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT "u"."name" AS "userName", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = shipped) AS "shippedCount" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true;

cases.name: select two scalar subqueries and a where value
cases.note: Extends the ordering contract to three params across two subqueries: p0 and p1 come from the two SELECT-list subqueries in list order, p2 from the WHERE. Pins that the walk is depth-first in emission order, not clause-type-grouped.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[state] = @p0) AS [openCount], (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o2] WHERE [o2].[state] = @p1) AS [closedCount] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] > @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 2, @p2 = 100;

mssql.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[state] = 1) AS [openCount], (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o2] WHERE [o2].[state] = 2) AS [closedCount] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] > 100;

prepared.sql: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`state` = ?) AS `openCount`, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o2` WHERE `o2`.`state` = ?) AS `closedCount` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` > ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`state` = 1) AS `openCount`, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o2` WHERE `o2`.`state` = 2) AS `closedCount` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` > 100;

prepared.sql: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."state" = $1) AS "openCount", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o2" WHERE "o2"."state" = $2) AS "closedCount" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" > $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."state" = 1) AS "openCount", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o2" WHERE "o2"."state" = 2) AS "closedCount" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" > 100;

prepared.sql: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."state" = ?) AS "openCount", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o2" WHERE "o2"."state" = ?) AS "closedCount" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" > ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."state" = 1) AS "openCount", (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o2" WHERE "o2"."state" = 2) AS "closedCount" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" > 100;

cases.name: select scalar subquery carries no trailing semicolon
cases.note: The SELECT-list subquery is rendered by a fresh defaultToSql with `isInnerStatement` set, and that flag's only remaining job here is to suppress the trailing `;` — the statement has exactly one, at the very end, and a port that forgets the flag emits a stray semicolon inside the parentheses on every dialect. Until 7.0.0 the flag did more: it kept MSSQL's safety-net `TOP (1000)` out of the subquery while the unbounded outer SELECT collected one, so a port that missed it emitted `(SELECT TOP (1000) COUNT(*) ...)` and silently changed the aggregate's meaning. The cap is gone on all four dialects, the MSSQL golden is now the same shape as the other three, and the semicolon is the whole test.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o]) AS [orderCount] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o]) AS [orderCount] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];

prepared.sql: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o`) AS `orderCount` FROM `users` AS `u`;

mysql.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `orders` AS `o`) AS `orderCount` FROM `users` AS `u`;

prepared.sql: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o") AS "orderCount" FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

postgres.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."orders" AS "o") AS "orderCount" FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

prepared.sql: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o") AS "orderCount" FROM "users" AS "u";

sqlite.raw: SELECT *, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "orders" AS "o") AS "orderCount" FROM "users" AS "u";

cases.name: select column name embedding every dialect delimiter
cases.note: Identifier escaping is per-dialect and doubles ONLY that dialect's own closing delimiter: MSSQL doubles `]`, MySQL doubles the backtick, Postgres and SQLite double `"`. The other two delimiter characters pass through untouched in each dialect's output — so the same input column name yields four genuinely different strings. Getting this wrong is an injection hole, not a cosmetic bug: an unescaped closing delimiter lets a hostile column or alias name break out of the quoting.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[we]]ird"na`me] AS [al]]ia"s`x] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT [u].[we]]ird"na`me] AS [al]]ia"s`x] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`we]ird"na``me` AS `al]ia"s``x` FROM `users` AS `u`;

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`we]ird"na``me` AS `al]ia"s``x` FROM `users` AS `u`;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."we]ird""na`me" AS "al]ia""s`x" FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."we]ird""na`me" AS "al]ia""s`x" FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."we]ird""na`me" AS "al]ia""s`x" FROM "users" AS "u";

sqlite.raw: SELECT "u"."we]ird""na`me" AS "al]ia""s`x" FROM "users" AS "u";

cases.name: select column name with a nul byte is refused
cases.note: A NUL can silently truncate an identifier inside some drivers, turning `secret\u0000junk` into `secret`, so it is rejected outright rather than escaped. Dart strings admit \u0000 just as JS does — a port that only guards the delimiter characters will pass this through.

cases.name: select with no from is refused
cases.note: Clause order in the error matters: SELECT is rendered before FROM, so a select-only builder gets past the select parser and dies in the FROM parser ('No tables to select from'). A port that validates FROM eagerly, or that validates in a different order, will surface a different message and fail the substring match.

cases.name: from with no select is refused
cases.note: The mirror image of the previous case: SELECT is validated first, so a FROM with an empty select list dies in the SELECT parser, never reaching FROM.

cases.name: from table without an alias
cases.note: The corpus models 'no alias' as the empty string because the TypeScript API uses '' as the sentinel. A Dart port with a nullable `alias` must treat null and '' identically: the ` AS "x"` suffix is emitted only when the alias is non-empty. Note the default owner (dbo/public) is still injected — omitting the alias does not omit the schema.

cases.name: from table with an explicit owner
cases.note: MySQL THROWS here while the other three render a qualified name. The check lives in the FROM parser, not the builder, so the failure surfaces at parse time — a Dart port that validates eagerly in `fromTableWithOwner()` would throw at the wrong moment and fail any test that builds-then-inspects. MSSQL/Postgres/SQLite all replace their default owner with the supplied one.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [sales].[orders] AS [o];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [sales].[orders] AS [o];

mysql.throws: From: MySQL does not support table owners

cases.name: from table with an explicit empty owner suppresses the default schema
cases.note: An explicit empty owner is NOT the same as no owner at all: `fromTable` substitutes the dialect default (dbo/public), whereas `fromTableWithOwner('')` emits an unqualified table on every dialect. This is also the only way to reach MySQL through the owner API without throwing. A Dart port that defaults a null/absent owner back to the dialect default here would emit [dbo].[users] and diverge.

cases.name: from table combined with raw fragments
cases.note: Raw FROM fragments are appended to the same source list as structured tables and joined with ', ' in insertion order — they are not a separate clause. The raw text is passed through verbatim: no quoting, no owner injection, identical in all four dialects.

cases.name: from derived table
cases.note: The derived table is rendered by the same parser, wrapped in parentheses, and marked as an inner statement — so there is no `;` inside the parens; the statement's single semicolon is the outer one. Before 7.0.0 this case also pinned that MSSQL's automatic `TOP (1000)` reached the OUTER select only and was never injected into a nested builder. There is no automatic TOP any more, so all four goldens are the same shape. For what a nested builder still does to an EXPLICIT `.top(n)`, see 'an explicit top inside a derived table is silently dropped' — that is this op-list plus a top, and its golden is byte-identical to this one.

cases.name: from derived table carrying a bound value
cases.note: The derived table's bound value must be spliced into the OUTER param list, ahead of the outer WHERE's value. If a port renders the subquery's SQL but drops its values, the placeholders still ship and every later parameter shifts by one — the query then binds NULL where it meant `true` and silently returns the wrong rows. Postgres is the loudest failure ($1/$2 renumbering); MySQL/SQLite fail silently.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0) AS [active_users] WHERE [active_users].[age] > @p1;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 21;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true) AS [active_users] WHERE [active_users].[age] > 21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ?) AS `active_users` WHERE `active_users`.`age` > ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true) AS `active_users` WHERE `active_users`.`age` > 21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1) AS "active_users" WHERE "active_users"."age" > $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true) AS "active_users" WHERE "active_users"."age" > 21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ?) AS "active_users" WHERE "active_users"."age" > ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true) AS "active_users" WHERE "active_users"."age" > 21;

cases.name: every join type in one query
cases.note: Pins the keyword text for all seven JoinTypes and the single-space separator between consecutive joins. `Left` and `LeftOuter` are DISTINCT: they render 'LEFT JOIN' and 'LEFT OUTER JOIN' respectively — a port that normalizes them to one keyword breaks byte equality. `Cross` with an empty ON list emits no ON clause at all; the SQL text is identical on all four dialects apart from identifier quoting and owner injection.

cases.name: join table with an explicit owner
cases.note: Deliberate asymmetry, and the reason this case exists: `fromTableWithOwner` throws on MySQL, but `joinTableWithOwner` does NOT — the owner guard lives only in the FROM parser. MySQL therefore emits `INNER JOIN `sales`.`orders``. A Dart port that hoists the guard into a shared helper (the obvious refactor) would start throwing here and diverge from the frozen behaviour. Reproduce the asymmetry; do not fix it.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [sales].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id];', N'';

cases.name: join on with or between two conditions
cases.note: AND/OR in an ON list are flat separators, not precedence-aware operators — no parentheses are inserted around the OR.

cases.name: join on value binds ahead of the where value
cases.note: `onValue` is the only ON form that binds a parameter. It is emitted in clause order, so 'active' is param 0 and the WHERE's 21 is param 1 — a port that collects WHERE values first would swap them and, on Postgres, produce `$1`/`$2` pointing at each other's values.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] AND [o].[status] = @p0 WHERE [u].[age] > @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'active', @p1 = 21;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] AND [o].[status] = active WHERE [u].[age] > 21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` AND `o`.`status` = ? WHERE `u`.`age` > ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` AND `o`.`status` = active WHERE `u`.`age` > 21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."status" = $1 WHERE "u"."age" > $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."status" = active WHERE "u"."age" > 21;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."status" = active WHERE "u"."age" > 21;

cases.name: join on group renders its conditions
cases.note: Regression. onGroup built its conditions into a child JoinOnBuilder and then discarded it, so the group rendered as an empty `()` and any onValue inside it was NEVER bound — the JOIN silently matched on the wrong predicate. The group must now contain its predicates, and its values must bind in emission order. Note the closing paren has no leading space: the group end suppresses the condition separator.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] AND ([o].[status] = [u].[default_status] OR [o].[type] = [u].[default_type]);', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] AND ([o].[status] = [u].[default_status] OR [o].[type] = [u].[default_type]);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` AND (`o`.`status` = `u`.`default_status` OR `o`.`type` = `u`.`default_type`);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND ("o"."status" = "u"."default_status" OR "o"."type" = "u"."default_type");

cases.name: bound value ordering across derived from join subquery on value and where
cases.note: The definitive parameter-ordering case for this area: four values entering from four different clauses. The contract is strict clause order — derived-FROM subquery (true), then the joined subquery (paid), then the join's onValue (100), then WHERE (21). Any port that gathers values per-op-type rather than per-rendered-position produces the right SQL with the wrong bindings, which no SQL syntax check will catch.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0) AS [au] INNER JOIN (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[status] = @p1) AS [ro] ON [au].[id] = [ro].[user_id] AND [ro].[total] > @p2 WHERE [au].[age] > @p3;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 tinyint, @p3 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'paid', @p2 = 100, @p3 = 21;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true) AS [au] INNER JOIN (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[status] = paid) AS [ro] ON [au].[id] = [ro].[user_id] AND [ro].[total] > 100 WHERE [au].[age] > 21;

cases.name: join on value with datetime null and double
cases.note: Exercises the value renderer through the JOIN path rather than WHERE, which is where MSSQL's `@pN <type>` declarations are derived. Two traps: (1) `onValue(null)` binds a NULL parameter and emits `= @p/?` — it is NOT rewritten to `IS NULL`, so a port must not special-case it; (2) the null still occupies a parameter slot, so dropping it (a natural Dart `where(notNull)` filter) shifts every later binding.

cases.name: where equals with a null value
cases.note: `where(..., Equals, null)` is NOT rewritten to IS NULL — it emits `= <placeholder>` and binds a null, which never matches a row. That is deliberate (the caller wanted whereNull), and each dialect renders it differently: MSSQL declares the parameter and assigns `@p0 = NULL`, the ?/$ dialects bind a real null so later parameters stay aligned, and raw mode inlines the null as the EMPTY STRING. A port that drops the null from params shifts every later value by one.

cases.name: where with a datetime value
cases.note: A DateTime is always rendered as its ISO-8601 UTC string with milliseconds and a trailing Z. MSSQL declares it nvarchar(max) (it falls through the type switch as an object) and single-quotes it WITHOUT the N prefix; the other dialects bind the value itself. Dart's DateTime.toIso8601String() only matches if the DateTime is UTC — a local-time DateTime emits no Z and produces a different string.

cases.name: where with integers spanning the mssql parameter type boundaries
cases.note: Only MSSQL diverges: it infers a T-SQL type per value. tinyint is UNSIGNED 0-255, so -1 must widen to smallint (declaring it tinyint made SQL Server raise arithmetic overflow). 256 -> smallint, 70000 -> int, 5000000000 -> bigint. A port that maps every integer to `int` or that keeps a signed -128..127 tinyint range fails here.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[zero] = @p0 AND [m].[tinyint_max] = @p1 AND [m].[negative] = @p2 AND [m].[smallint_range] = @p3 AND [m].[int_range] = @p4 AND [m].[bigint_range] = @p5;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 smallint, @p3 smallint, @p4 int, @p5 bigint', @p0 = 0, @p1 = 255, @p2 = -1, @p3 = 256, @p4 = 70000, @p5 = 5000000000;

cases.name: where between two datetimes with predicates on both sides
cases.note: BETWEEN emits TWO placeholders and binds low then high. It must not be collapsed into one, and its two values sit between the surrounding predicates' values in the params list — get the order wrong and the range silently inverts.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[events] AS [e] WHERE [e].[kind] = @p0 AND [e].[occurred_at] BETWEEN @p1 AND @p2 AND [e].[source] = @p3;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 nvarchar(max), @p3 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'signup', @p1 = '2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z', @p2 = '2024-12-31T23:59:59.999Z', @p3 = N'web';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[events] AS [e] WHERE [e].[kind] = signup AND [e].[occurred_at] BETWEEN 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z AND 2024-12-31T23:59:59.999Z AND [e].[source] = web;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `events` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`kind` = ? AND `e`.`occurred_at` BETWEEN ? AND ? AND `e`.`source` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `events` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`kind` = signup AND `e`.`occurred_at` BETWEEN 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z AND 2024-12-31T23:59:59.999Z AND `e`.`source` = web;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."kind" = $1 AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN $2 AND $3 AND "e"."source" = $4;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."kind" = signup AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z AND 2024-12-31T23:59:59.999Z AND "e"."source" = web;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."kind" = ? AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN ? AND ? AND "e"."source" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."kind" = signup AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z AND 2024-12-31T23:59:59.999Z AND "e"."source" = web;

cases.name: where null and where not null chained
cases.note: IS NULL / IS NOT NULL bind NOTHING. A port that pushes a placeholder for them shifts every later bound value — hence the trailing `= 1` here, whose parameter must still be the first and only one.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[deleted_at] IS NULL AND [u].[email] IS NOT NULL AND [u].[tenant_id] = @p0;', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[deleted_at] IS NULL AND [u].[email] IS NOT NULL AND [u].[tenant_id] = 1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."deleted_at" IS NULL AND "u"."email" IS NOT NULL AND "u"."tenant_id" = $1;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."deleted_at" IS NULL AND "u"."email" IS NOT NULL AND "u"."tenant_id" = 1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."deleted_at" IS NULL AND "u"."email" IS NOT NULL AND "u"."tenant_id" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."deleted_at" IS NULL AND "u"."email" IS NOT NULL AND "u"."tenant_id" = 1;

cases.name: where in values with mixed types including a null
cases.note: Every element of the list gets its own placeholder and its own bound value — including the null, and including the bool. MSSQL declares a DIFFERENT T-SQL type per element of the same list (tinyint / nvarchar(max) / bit / nvarchar(max) for the null). The separator is exactly `, `.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[u] AS [u] WHERE [u].[flag] IN (@p0, @p1, @p2, @p3);', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 bit, @p3 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'two', @p2 = 1, @p3 = NULL;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[u] AS [u] WHERE [u].[flag] IN (1, two, true, );

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `u` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`flag` IN (?, ?, ?, ?);

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `u` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`flag` IN (1, two, true, );

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."u" AS "u" WHERE "u"."flag" IN ($1, $2, $3, $4);

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."u" AS "u" WHERE "u"."flag" IN (1, two, true, );

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "u" AS "u" WHERE "u"."flag" IN (?, ?, ?, ?);

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "u" AS "u" WHERE "u"."flag" IN (1, two, true, );

cases.name: where raw with a literal question mark and dollar sign between bound values
cases.note: A raw WHERE fragment is passed through verbatim and must never be scanned for placeholders. The `?` inside '%why?%' is caller text, and so is the `$` in '$100'; a port that finds placeholders by scanning the rendered SQL for a bare ?/$ corrupts these literals and misaligns the bound params (Postgres turned '$100' into '$1100'). The two real bound values must still land as slots 1 and 2.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = @p0 AND u.notes LIKE ''%why?%'' AND u.price <> ''$100'' AND [u].[name] = @p1;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'after';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = 1 AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND [u].[name] = after;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` = ? AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND `u`.`name` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` = 1 AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND `u`.`name` = after;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = $1 AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND "u"."name" = $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 1 AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND "u"."name" = after;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = ? AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND "u"."name" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 1 AND u.notes LIKE '%why?%' AND u.price <> '$100' AND "u"."name" = after;

cases.note: Empty whereGroup is refused with ParserError (AF-05). Ports must throw rather than emit WHERE ().

cases.name: nested where groups two levels deep
cases.note: Spacing is the trap. A group renders as `(`, the sub-builder's WHERE body with its `WHERE ` prefix stripped, then `)` — and no space is emitted between the last inner predicate and the closing paren. Get it wrong and you get `( ... )` instead of `(...)`. Params bind depth-first in emission order: 1, 2, 3, 4.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE ([u].[a] = @p0 AND ([u].[b] = @p1 OR [u].[c] = @p2)) OR [u].[d] = @p3;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint, @p3 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 2, @p2 = 3, @p3 = 4;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE ([u].[a] = 1 AND ([u].[b] = 2 OR [u].[c] = 3)) OR [u].[d] = 4;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE (`u`.`a` = ? AND (`u`.`b` = ? OR `u`.`c` = ?)) OR `u`.`d` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE (`u`.`a` = 1 AND (`u`.`b` = 2 OR `u`.`c` = 3)) OR `u`.`d` = 4;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE ("u"."a" = $1 AND ("u"."b" = $2 OR "u"."c" = $3)) OR "u"."d" = $4;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE ("u"."a" = 1 AND ("u"."b" = 2 OR "u"."c" = 3)) OR "u"."d" = 4;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE ("u"."a" = ? AND ("u"."b" = ? OR "u"."c" = ?)) OR "u"."d" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE ("u"."a" = 1 AND ("u"."b" = 2 OR "u"."c" = 3)) OR "u"."d" = 4;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE ("u"."role" = admin AND "u"."email" IS NOT NULL) OR ("u"."role" = owner AND "u"."age" >= 21);

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE ("u"."role" = admin AND "u"."email" IS NOT NULL) OR ("u"."role" = owner AND "u"."age" >= 21);

cases.name: where in with builder followed by an outer predicate
cases.note: Regression class: the subquery's value used to be dropped from params entirely, leaving `true` bound to slot 1. The subquery's 100 must occupy slot 1 and the outer true slot 2 — placeholders bind in EMISSION order, not in the order the builder methods were called.

cases.note: The subquery's value must land in the MIDDLE slot (18, 'spam', 'a%'). Collecting outer values first and subquery values afterwards — an easy way to write the parser — produces the right SQL with the wrong bindings, which no raw-SQL assertion would catch.

cases.name: where exists with builder correlated by a raw predicate
cases.note: EXISTS ignores the table/column arguments entirely — the output is `EXISTS (subquery)`, with no column reference before it. A port that renders the column (e.g. `[u].[id] EXISTS (...)`) diverges. The correlated raw fragment is emitted verbatim, and the subquery's 50 binds between the outer true and 65.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND [o].[total] > @p1) AND [u].[age] < @p2;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 50, @p2 = 65;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND [o].[total] > 50) AND [u].[age] < 65;

cases.name: where group containing an in subquery with values on both sides
cases.note: The parameter-order case that matters most: a subquery nested inside a GROUP, which is itself a sub-builder. Values must come out 1, 100, true, 21 — the group's own values are spliced into the parent at the point the group is emitted, and the subquery's values are spliced into the group at the point IT is emitted. Any parser that gathers group values or subquery values in a separate pass gets identical SQL with permuted bindings.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[tenant_id] = @p0 AND ([u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > @p1) OR [u].[vip] = @p2) AND [u].[age] >= @p3;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 bit, @p3 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 100, @p2 = 1, @p3 = 21;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`tenant_id` = 1 AND (`u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > 100) OR `u`.`vip` = true) AND `u`.`age` >= 21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."tenant_id" = $1 AND ("u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > $2) OR "u"."vip" = $3) AND "u"."age" >= $4;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."tenant_id" = 1 AND ("u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 100) OR "u"."vip" = true) AND "u"."age" >= 21;

cases.name: in subquery nested two levels deep
cases.note: Two levels of sub-builder. Params must be 100, 10, true: inner-most first, because it is emitted first. Postgres numbering ($1..$3) is the visible proof — a port that renumbers per sub-builder emits $1 twice.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > @p0 AND [o].[product_id] IN (SELECT [p].[id] FROM [dbo].[products] AS [p] WHERE [p].[price] < @p1)) AND [u].[active] = @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 bit', @p0 = 100, @p1 = 10, @p2 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > 100 AND [o].[product_id] IN (SELECT [p].[id] FROM [dbo].[products] AS [p] WHERE [p].[price] < 10)) AND [u].[active] = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > ? AND `o`.`product_id` IN (SELECT `p`.`id` FROM `products` AS `p` WHERE `p`.`price` < ?)) AND `u`.`active` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > 100 AND `o`.`product_id` IN (SELECT `p`.`id` FROM `products` AS `p` WHERE `p`.`price` < 10)) AND `u`.`active` = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > $1 AND "o"."product_id" IN (SELECT "p"."id" FROM "public"."products" AS "p" WHERE "p"."price" < $2)) AND "u"."active" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 100 AND "o"."product_id" IN (SELECT "p"."id" FROM "public"."products" AS "p" WHERE "p"."price" < 10)) AND "u"."active" = true;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > ? AND "o"."product_id" IN (SELECT "p"."id" FROM "products" AS "p" WHERE "p"."price" < ?)) AND "u"."active" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 100 AND "o"."product_id" IN (SELECT "p"."id" FROM "products" AS "p" WHERE "p"."price" < 10)) AND "u"."active" = true;

cases.name: and as the first operator inside a where group is refused
cases.note: Subtle: the group body is its own sub-builder, so a leading AND inside it is caught as `First WHERE operator cannot be AND or OR` — NOT as `AND or OR cannot be used directly after a group begin`. The message is part of the contract; a port that emits the group-begin message here diverges. (The group-begin check exists but is unreachable through this API, since a group begin is always followed by the group's body node.)

cases.name: trailing and inside a where group is refused
cases.note: The group body is validated by the same walk as a top-level WHERE, so a dangling AND inside the parentheses must throw even though the OUTER chain is well-formed. A port that only validates the outer list emits `(a = 1 AND )`.

cases.name: empty not in values is refused
cases.note: `NOT IN ()` is a syntax error in every dialect. It is refused rather than rewritten to a true predicate, because an empty collection is nearly always a caller bug. The message differs from the IN one — it says NOT IN.

cases.name: group by two columns and a raw expression
cases.note: Two things were pinned here; one survives. (1) GROUP BY entries are joined with ', ' in insertion order regardless of kind, so a raw fragment interleaves with quoted columns exactly where it was added — `DATE(o.created_at)` lands last, unquoted and verbatim. (2) Before 7.0.0 the second pin was that a GROUP BY did not count as 'bounded': with no WHERE, MSSQL's unbounded-query safety net still fired and stacked a `TOP (1000)` on top of the aggregate while the other three emitted nothing. That cap is gone, all four goldens are now the same shape, and only (1) is under test.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [o].[status], [o].[region], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] GROUP BY [o].[status], [o].[region], DATE(o.created_at);', N'';

postgres.raw: SELECT "o"."status", "o"."region", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" GROUP BY "o"."status", "o"."region", DATE(o.created_at);

cases.name: group by with having greater than
cases.note: Param ORDER is the contract: the WHERE value binds before the HAVING value, because to-sql walks WHERE then GROUP BY then HAVING. A port that collects HAVING params first still produces identical SQL text but binds them in the wrong order — MySQL/SQLite would silently compare `active > 5`, and MSSQL would declare @p0 tinyint / @p1 bit. Nothing catches this except the params array.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[status] AS [status], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 GROUP BY [u].[status] HAVING [u].[status] > @p1;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 5;

mssql.raw: SELECT [u].[status] AS [status], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true GROUP BY [u].[status] HAVING [u].[status] > 5;

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`status` AS `status`, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? GROUP BY `u`.`status` HAVING `u`.`status` > ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`status` AS `status`, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true GROUP BY `u`.`status` HAVING `u`.`status` > 5;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."status" AS "status", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 GROUP BY "u"."status" HAVING "u"."status" > $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."status" AS "status", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true GROUP BY "u"."status" HAVING "u"."status" > 5;

cases.name: having without group by is refused
cases.note: HAVING is only legal against a GROUP BY here, and the check lives in the HAVING parser — not the builder — so it fires at parse time, not at .having() time. A port that validates eagerly in the builder throws too early and cannot reproduce the message.

cases.name: order by direction none renders desc
cases.note: The trap in ORDER BY. The direction enum has three members (Ascending, Descending, None) but the parser tests only `direction == Ascending` and falls to DESC for everything else — so None renders DESC, not ASC and not a bare column. A Dart port that maps None to the dialect default, or that defaults an unset direction to ASC, silently reverses the sort order of every such query.

cases.name: limit only without an order by
cases.note: Highest-value divergence in this area, and the one that pins the top()/limit() split. MySQL/Postgres/SQLite emit `LIMIT 10` — legal and unordered. MSSQL renders EVERY limit as OFFSET/FETCH pagination, and T-SQL accepts OFFSET/FETCH only alongside an ORDER BY (Msg 102), so MSSQL REJECTS this outright and the case pins an error rather than SQL. `.limit()` is pagination and needs an order to page against; `.top(n)` is the designer's manual, SQL-Server-only row cap and the tool for an unordered one. A port that quietly emits `TOP (10)` here instead of throwing has conflated the two, and produces a query the caller never asked for. Since 7.0.0 that guard is the only thing standing between this op-list and a TOP: before, a port could have explained the missing TOP away as the automatic cap's explicit-limit suppression rule, which no longer exists.

mssql.throws: LimitOffset: ORDER BY is required when using LIMIT on MSSQL, which paginates with OFFSET/FETCH; use top() for an unordered row cap

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` LIMIT 10;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` LIMIT 10;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" LIMIT 10;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" LIMIT 10;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" LIMIT 10;

cases.name: limit and offset with an order by
cases.note: Byte-exact whitespace matters: the LIMIT..OFFSET dialects emit a DOUBLE space — `LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20` — because the emitter appends a separator space and then a snippet that already begins with one. Ugly, and frozen. MSSQL instead emits `OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY`, i.e. the two numbers appear in the OPPOSITE order to LIMIT..OFFSET; swapping them is an easy port bug that still produces valid SQL and wrong pages.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

cases.name: offset only with an order by and no where clause
cases.note: Offset without a limit, and the four grammars that disagree about how to spell it. Postgres is the only dialect with a legal standalone OFFSET, so it emits a bare `  OFFSET 20` — note the LEADING double space, deliberate and pinned in detail by 'offset with a where and no limit: only postgres keeps the doubled space'. MySQL and SQLite cannot parse a bare OFFSET at all (ERROR 1064 / `near "OFFSET"`), so each emits its own "no upper bound" sentinel limit in front of it: `LIMIT 18446744073709551615` (2^64-1, the documented MySQL idiom — 2^64 is itself a syntax error) and `LIMIT -1`. Those sentinels are grammar, not a cap: they bound nothing, and they are followed by a single space because the extra separator is only added when an explicit limit was set. MSSQL emits `OFFSET 20 ROWS` with no FETCH, which is valid T-SQL. One intent, four spellings; a port that picks one and applies it everywhere breaks three dialects. Two dead contracts a port must not resurrect: 6.x synthesized a `LIMIT 1000` / `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY` cap here because there was no WHERE, and before 6.0.0 MSSQL emitted `TOP (1000) ... OFFSET 20 ROWS`, which SQL Server never accepted. 7.0.0 removed the cap outright.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS;', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 20;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 20;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 20;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 20;

cases.name: offset only with a where clause renders exactly as it does without one
cases.note: The companion to 'offset only with an order by and no where clause' — the same op-list plus a WHERE. Compare the two goldens: the limit/offset tails are byte-identical on all four dialects, and that is now the entire point. Until 7.0.0 a WHERE was what marked a query 'bounded' and suppressed the automatic row cap, so the two cases had different tails everywhere: no WHERE meant `LIMIT 1000` / `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY`, a WHERE meant the sentinel-or-bare-OFFSET rendering seen here. 7.0.0 removed the cap, and with it the only reason defaultLimitOffset ever read `whereStates`. A port must not carry a WHERE check into its limit/offset code at all: nothing about pagination looks at the predicate now.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 20;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 20;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ? ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 20;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 20;

cases.note: Thrown by the limit/offset parser for all four dialects, and it fires even though a limit is also set. The guard is evaluated AFTER the clause text is built, so a port that returns early on the MSSQL branch never reaches it.

cases.note: limit(0) and negative limits throw ParserError (M1).

cases.name: explicit mssql top

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];

cases.name: mssql top with a where clause
cases.note: TOP and WHERE in one statement: the TOP goes in through the select hook and lands before the column list, the WHERE is appended after the FROM — `SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0`. Until 7.0.0 the case earned its keep by pinning a precedence: an explicit TOP beat both the automatic cap and the WHERE-suppresses-the-cap rule, so `TOP (5)` survived in a position where `TOP (1000)` would have been suppressed. With the cap gone there is no precedence left to resolve — an explicit top renders whenever it is set and positive, nothing else ever writes a TOP, and the WHERE is irrelevant to it.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true;

cases.name: mssql top combined with limit is refused
cases.note: defaultLimitOffset throws when MSSQL has both a `top` in customState and a limit/offset: they are two different pagination mechanisms and T-SQL rejects them in one SELECT (Msg 10741). The subtle pin is WHICH error. This op-list trips two guards — it has a limit and no ORDER BY, so MSSQL's 'ORDER BY is required when using LIMIT' guard is eligible too — but the TOP/LIMIT conflict is raised while the clause text is still being built and the ORDER BY guards only after it, so the TOP/LIMIT message is the one a port must produce. Note also that the conflict check reads customState presence, not the rendered SQL, so a port must track 'top was set by the caller' as its own state; see 'top zero is absent for rendering but present for the limit conflict check', where a top that renders nothing still throws. (The pre-7.0.0 note here justified the presence test by claiming the implicit TOP (1000) coexisted with an OFFSET elsewhere in the corpus and therefore never threw. That was never true — MSSQL spelled the cap `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY` precisely because TOP and OFFSET cannot share a SELECT. The presence test is right for the reason above, not that one.)

mssql.throws: LimitOffset: MSSQL should not use both TOP and LIMIT/OFFSET in the same query

cases.name: top is silently ignored by the non-mssql dialects
cases.note: top() lives on the shared builder, not on the MSSQL builder, so it is callable everywhere — and everywhere else it is a no-op: no TOP, no LIMIT, no clause at all. The golden is byte-identical to the seed case. A Dart port that throws 'unsupported' on top() for MySQL, or that translates it into LIMIT, diverges. It also must not throw the TOP+LIMIT error outside MSSQL — that guard lives inside the MSSQL branch of defaultLimitOffset. (The old note explained the empty tail by saying these three dialects had no unbounded-query safety net, unlike MSSQL. Since 7.0.0 no dialect has one: an unbounded SELECT is unbounded everywhere unless the caller caps it.)

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u`;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u`;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u";

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u";

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u";

cases.name: subquery with group by having order by and limit
cases.note: An EXPLICIT limit inside a subquery is rendered, and rendered per dialect: `LIMIT 5` on MySQL/Postgres/SQLite, `OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 5 ROWS ONLY` on MSSQL — note MSSQL pays for a limit with an OFFSET the caller never gave, because T-SQL has no FETCH without one. The subquery's orderByRaw is what makes that legal: MSSQL's OFFSET/FETCH requires an ORDER BY (Msg 102) and the guard reads the inner state, so dropping the inner order by would turn this case into a throw. The IN subquery is an inner statement and carries no trailing `;`, so the statement has exactly one, at the very end. (Before 7.0.0 the note also said inner statements were exempt from the automatic cap, and that the outer query's IN counted as a WHERE and so 'bounded' it. Both rules are gone: no statement gets a cap, inner or outer, and nothing reads whereStates for pagination.)

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] GROUP BY [o].[user_id] HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 5 ROWS ONLY);', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] GROUP BY [o].[user_id] HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 5 ROWS ONLY);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` GROUP BY `o`.`user_id` HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 5);

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` GROUP BY `o`.`user_id` HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 5);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" GROUP BY "o"."user_id" HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 5);

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" GROUP BY "o"."user_id" HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 5);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" GROUP BY "o"."user_id" HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 5);

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" GROUP BY "o"."user_id" HAVING COUNT(*) > 3 ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 5);

cases.name: full clause order: where group by having order by limit offset
cases.note: Pins the clause emission order end to end — SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT/OFFSET — and, with it, the bound-param order (WHERE value first, HAVING value second). This is the case that fails loudly if a port renders HAVING before GROUP BY or appends LIMIT before ORDER BY.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[status] AS [status], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 GROUP BY [u].[status] HAVING [u].[status] >= @p1 ORDER BY cnt DESC OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT [u].[status] AS [status], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true GROUP BY [u].[status] HAVING [u].[status] >= 1 ORDER BY cnt DESC OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`status` AS `status`, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? GROUP BY `u`.`status` HAVING `u`.`status` >= ? ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`status` AS `status`, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true GROUP BY `u`.`status` HAVING `u`.`status` >= 1 ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."status" AS "status", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 GROUP BY "u"."status" HAVING "u"."status" >= $2 ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."status" AS "status", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true GROUP BY "u"."status" HAVING "u"."status" >= 1 ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."status" AS "status", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ? GROUP BY "u"."status" HAVING "u"."status" >= ? ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

sqlite.raw: SELECT "u"."status" AS "status", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true GROUP BY "u"."status" HAVING "u"."status" >= 1 ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

cases.name: insert single row binds every value in column order
cases.note: The value list binds positionally against the column list: name, age, score, active. MSSQL also declares a per-parameter T-SQL type inferred from each value — int 30 becomes tinyint, double 9.99 becomes float, bool becomes bit, string becomes nvarchar(max) — so a port that mis-tags a value emits a different @pN declaration and SQL Server rejects or silently coerces the row.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [age], [score], [active]) VALUES (@p0, @p1, @p2, @p3);', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint, @p2 float, @p3 bit', @p0 = N'Alice', @p1 = 30, @p2 = 9.99, @p3 = 1;

mssql.raw: INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [age], [score], [active]) VALUES (Alice, 30, 9.99, true);

cases.note: MySQL rejects non-empty table owners on FROM, JOIN, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE — same guard across clauses. Other dialects emit the qualified name.

A port that treats an empty/null owner as 'unset' and falls back to the default schema writes to the wrong table.

cases.name: insert with null datetime and quoted string values
cases.note: The NULL still occupies a placeholder slot: the bound params are [datetime, null, string]. A port that filters nulls out of the value list when collecting bound params shifts every later parameter one position left and writes values into the wrong columns while still executing cleanly. MSSQL declares both the Date and the null as nvarchar(max), inlines the date as a quoted ISO-8601 string and the null as the bare word NULL, and doubles the apostrophe in the string. parseRaw inlines the null as the EMPTY STRING — debug rendering only, never executable.

cases.name: insert raw discards a previously built insert
cases.note: insertRaw short-circuits the whole INSERT parser: once insertState.raw is set, the table, columns and values built before it are silently dropped (src/parser/default-insert.ts returns immediately). Nothing warns. A port that appends the raw fragment to the generated INSERT, or that lets the earlier state win, emits a completely different statement.

cases.name: insert values without insert into is refused
cases.note: insertColumns/insertValues do NOT make the builder an INSERT — only insertInto/insertIntoWithOwner/insertRaw set the query type. Without one, the builder is still a SELECT and fails in the SELECT parser, which is why the error mentions select state and not INSERT. The parser's own 'No insert state provided' guard is unreachable through the public API. Pinned so a port fails the same way with the same message.

cases.name: update with alias and one set
cases.note: The headline UPDATE divergence. T-SQL has no `UPDATE tbl AS alias`, so MSSQL emits `UPDATE [u] SET ... FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]` — the alias alone after UPDATE, the real table in a trailing FROM. Every other dialect emits `UPDATE tbl AS u SET ...` and no FROM. Get this wrong on MSSQL and the statement does not parse at all.
