sqlite.raw: UPDATE "sales"."orders" AS "o" SET "status" = shipped;

cases.name: update set values bind before where values
cases.note: THE ordering pin for UPDATE. SET values are emitted before WHERE values, so they bind first: Postgres numbers the SET values $1, $2 and the WHERE values $3, $4, and the positional dialects bind [John, 31, 42, 'deleted'] in exactly that order. setRaw contributes NO bound value and NO placeholder — it must not consume an index. A port that renders WHERE before SET, or that counts setRaw as a parameter, produces an UPDATE that writes the wrong values to the wrong rows and still executes without error.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [u] SET [name] = @p0, login_count = login_count + 1, [age] = @p1 FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = @p2 AND [u].[status] <> @p3;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint, @p3 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'John', @p1 = 31, @p2 = 42, @p3 = N'deleted';

mssql.raw: UPDATE [u] SET [name] = John, login_count = login_count + 1, [age] = 31 FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = 42 AND [u].[status] <> deleted;

prepared.sql: UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `name` = ?, login_count = login_count + 1, `age` = ? WHERE `u`.`id` = ? AND `u`.`status` <> ?;

mysql.raw: UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `name` = John, login_count = login_count + 1, `age` = 31 WHERE `u`.`id` = 42 AND `u`.`status` <> deleted;

prepared.sql: UPDATE "public"."users" AS "u" SET "name" = $1, login_count = login_count + 1, "age" = $2 WHERE "u"."id" = $3 AND "u"."status" <> $4;

postgres.raw: UPDATE "public"."users" AS "u" SET "name" = John, login_count = login_count + 1, "age" = 31 WHERE "u"."id" = 42 AND "u"."status" <> deleted;

prepared.sql: UPDATE "users" AS "u" SET "name" = ?, login_count = login_count + 1, "age" = ? WHERE "u"."id" = ? AND "u"."status" <> ?;

sqlite.raw: UPDATE "users" AS "u" SET "name" = John, login_count = login_count + 1, "age" = 31 WHERE "u"."id" = 42 AND "u"."status" <> deleted;

cases.name: update set null keeps its placeholder slot
cases.note: Setting a column to NULL binds a null parameter — it is not skipped and not rendered as a literal NULL in the SET list. Params are [null, datetime, 7]: drop the null and the datetime binds to deleted_at and the id binds to updated_at, so the UPDATE hits every row it should not. See the 'never filter' comment in SqlHelper.getValues.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [u] SET [deleted_at] = @p0, [updated_at] = @p1 FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 tinyint', @p0 = NULL, @p1 = '2024-06-01T08:30:00.000Z', @p2 = 7;

mssql.raw: UPDATE [u] SET [deleted_at] = , [updated_at] = 2024-06-01T08:30:00.000Z FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = 7;

prepared.sql: UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `deleted_at` = ?, `updated_at` = ? WHERE `u`.`id` = ?;

mysql.raw: UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `deleted_at` = , `updated_at` = 2024-06-01T08:30:00.000Z WHERE `u`.`id` = 7;

prepared.sql: UPDATE "public"."users" AS "u" SET "deleted_at" = $1, "updated_at" = $2 WHERE "u"."id" = $3;

postgres.raw: UPDATE "public"."users" AS "u" SET "deleted_at" = , "updated_at" = 2024-06-01T08:30:00.000Z WHERE "u"."id" = 7;

prepared.sql: UPDATE "users" AS "u" SET "deleted_at" = ?, "updated_at" = ? WHERE "u"."id" = ?;

sqlite.raw: UPDATE "users" AS "u" SET "deleted_at" = , "updated_at" = 2024-06-01T08:30:00.000Z WHERE "u"."id" = 7;

cases.name: update with no set columns is refused

mssql.throws: Update: UPDATE requires at least one SET column

mysql.throws: Update: UPDATE requires at least one SET column

postgres.throws: Update: UPDATE requires at least one SET column

sqlite.throws: Update: UPDATE requires at least one SET column

cases.name: update set without update table is refused
cases.note: set()/setRaw() do not make the builder an UPDATE — only updateTable/updateTableWithOwner do, and both push the table at the same time. So the parser's 'UPDATE requires a table' guard (and the identical DELETE one) is unreachable through the public API: omit updateTable and the builder is still a SELECT, failing in the SELECT parser. The message pinned here is the one a port must reproduce — not a friendlier 'UPDATE requires a table'.

mssql.throws: Select: Select statement must have at least one select state

mysql.throws: Select: Select statement must have at least one select state

postgres.throws: Select: Select statement must have at least one select state

sqlite.throws: Select: Select statement must have at least one select state

cases.name: delete with alias and where
cases.note: The headline DELETE divergence, mirroring UPDATE: T-SQL has no `DELETE FROM tbl AS alias`, so MSSQL emits `DELETE [u] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE ...` while the other three emit `DELETE FROM tbl AS u WHERE ...`. The Delete branch of to-sql returns as soon as it has written the WHERE and the semicolon, so nothing downstream of it — the select hook, limit/offset — can reach a write statement at all. That structural early return, not a rule about writes, is why MSSQL's TOP has never appeared on a DELETE. (Before 7.0.0 the same early return was also what kept the automatic `TOP (1000)` off an unfiltered DELETE.)

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'DELETE [u] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = @p0;', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 42;

mssql.raw: DELETE [u] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = 42;

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` = ?;

mysql.raw: DELETE FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` = 42;

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = $1;

postgres.raw: DELETE FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 42;

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = ?;

sqlite.raw: DELETE FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" = 42;

cases.name: delete without alias
cases.note: With no alias every dialect — MSSQL included — takes the plain `DELETE FROM <table>` form. This is an unfiltered DELETE and the builder emits it without complaint: there is no guard requiring a WHERE.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'DELETE FROM [dbo].[users];', N'';

mssql.raw: DELETE FROM [dbo].[users];

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM `users`;

mysql.raw: DELETE FROM `users`;

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "public"."users";

postgres.raw: DELETE FROM "public"."users";

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "users";

sqlite.raw: DELETE FROM "users";

cases.name: delete with owner and where in values
cases.note: The IN list binds one parameter per value, after the table is rendered; MySQL again emits the owner instead of rejecting it (see 'insert into with owner').

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'DELETE [u] FROM [archive].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (@p0, @p1);', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 2;

mssql.raw: DELETE [u] FROM [archive].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (1, 2);

mysql.throws: Delete: MySQL does not support table owners

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "archive"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN ($1, $2);

postgres.raw: DELETE FROM "archive"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (1, 2);

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "archive"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (?, ?);

sqlite.raw: DELETE FROM "archive"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (1, 2);

cases.name: cte with a bound value binds before the outer query
cases.note: Pins parameter ORDER, not just SQL shape. The WITH clause is emitted first, so its values occupy the first placeholder slots: Postgres numbers the CTE's value $1 and the outer WHERE's value $2. A port that walks the outer clauses before the CTE still produces identical-looking SQL but binds the values swapped — the query silently returns wrong rows instead of failing.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE [au].[age] > @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'active', @p1 = 18;

mssql.raw: WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE [au].[age] > 18;

prepared.sql: WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = ?) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE `au`.`age` > ?;

mysql.raw: WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE `au`.`age` > 18;

prepared.sql: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = $1) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."age" > $2;

postgres.raw: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."age" > 18;

prepared.sql: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = ?) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."age" > ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."age" > 18;

cases.name: two ctes each carrying a value bind in declaration order
cases.note: Extends the single-CTE ordering rule: CTE values bind in the order the CTEs were declared, all of them ahead of the outer query's. Expected Postgres numbering is $1 (first CTE), $2 (second CTE), $3 (outer WHERE).

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0), [big_orders] AS (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > @p1) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE [au].[id] > @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 float, @p2 tinyint', @p0 = N'active', @p1 = 99.5, @p2 = 0;

mssql.raw: WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active), [big_orders] AS (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > 99.5) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE [au].[id] > 0;

prepared.sql: WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT `u`.`id` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = ?), `big_orders` AS (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > ?) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE `au`.`id` > ?;

mysql.raw: WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT `u`.`id` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = active), `big_orders` AS (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > 99.5) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE `au`.`id` > 0;

prepared.sql: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT "u"."id" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = $1), "big_orders" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > $2) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > $3;

postgres.raw: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT "u"."id" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active), "big_orders" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 99.5) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > 0;

prepared.sql: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT "u"."id" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = ?), "big_orders" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > ?) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT "u"."id" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active), "big_orders" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 99.5) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > 0;

cases.name: recursive cte with a union all body
cases.note: The canonical recursive shape: anchor UNION ALL recursive member, inside the CTE body. (1) Non-MSSQL dialects emit `WITH RECURSIVE`; MSSQL emits bare `WITH` because `RECURSIVE` is not a T-SQL keyword. (2) Value order inside the body: the anchor's value binds before the recursive member's, and both bind before any outer value.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [descendants] AS (SELECT [e].[id], [e].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e] WHERE [e].[id] = @p0 UNION ALL SELECT [e2].[id], [e2].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e2] WHERE [e2].[depth] < @p1) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE [d].[id] <> @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 10, @p2 = 1;

mssql.raw: WITH [descendants] AS (SELECT [e].[id], [e].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e] WHERE [e].[id] = 1 UNION ALL SELECT [e2].[id], [e2].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e2] WHERE [e2].[depth] < 10) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE [d].[id] <> 1;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE `descendants` AS (SELECT `e`.`id`, `e`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`id` = ? UNION ALL SELECT `e2`.`id`, `e2`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e2` WHERE `e2`.`depth` < ?) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE `d`.`id` <> ?;

mysql.raw: WITH RECURSIVE `descendants` AS (SELECT `e`.`id`, `e`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`id` = 1 UNION ALL SELECT `e2`.`id`, `e2`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e2` WHERE `e2`.`depth` < 10) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE `d`.`id` <> 1;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE "descendants" AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = $1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e2" WHERE "e2"."depth" < $2) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE "d"."id" <> $3;

postgres.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "descendants" AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = 1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e2" WHERE "e2"."depth" < 10) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE "d"."id" <> 1;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE "descendants" AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = ? UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e2" WHERE "e2"."depth" < ?) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE "d"."id" <> ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "descendants" AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = 1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e2" WHERE "e2"."depth" < 10) SELECT * FROM descendants AS d WHERE "d"."id" <> 1;

cases.name: one recursive cte promotes the whole with clause to recursive
cases.note: RECURSIVE is a property of the WITH clause, not of an individual CTE: a single cteRecursive anywhere in the list makes the clause recursive even though the first CTE here is not recursive. Non-MSSQL dialects emit `WITH RECURSIVE`; MSSQL emits bare `WITH`. Declaration order is preserved — the recursive one is NOT hoisted to the front.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [base] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[categories] AS [c]), [tree] AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE [t].[depth] <= @p0;', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 3;

mssql.raw: WITH [base] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[categories] AS [c]), [tree] AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE [t].[depth] <= 3;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE `base` AS (SELECT * FROM `categories` AS `c`), `tree` AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE `t`.`depth` <= ?;

mysql.raw: WITH RECURSIVE `base` AS (SELECT * FROM `categories` AS `c`), `tree` AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE `t`.`depth` <= 3;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE "base" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."categories" AS "c"), "tree" AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE "t"."depth" <= $1;

postgres.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "base" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."categories" AS "c"), "tree" AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE "t"."depth" <= 3;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE "base" AS (SELECT * FROM "categories" AS "c"), "tree" AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE "t"."depth" <= ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "base" AS (SELECT * FROM "categories" AS "c"), "tree" AS (SELECT * FROM base AS b) SELECT * FROM tree AS t WHERE "t"."depth" <= 3;

cases.name: cte raw body is emitted verbatim
cases.note: cteRaw text is passed through untouched — not quoted, not escaped, not scanned for placeholders — while the CTE NAME is still quoted with the dialect's identifier delimiters. The embedded single quotes survive into the MSSQL sp_executesql string as doubled quotes, because the whole statement is escaped once at the end.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [constants] AS (SELECT 1 AS id, ''seed'' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;', N'';

mssql.raw: WITH [constants] AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

prepared.sql: WITH `constants` AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

mysql.raw: WITH `constants` AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

prepared.sql: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

postgres.raw: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

prepared.sql: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

sqlite.raw: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS id, 'seed' AS label) SELECT * FROM constants AS c;

cases.name: cte raw before a cte builder does not consume a placeholder
cases.note: A raw CTE contributes SQL but no bound values, so it must not shift placeholder numbering. The builder CTE that follows it still takes the first slot ($1 in Postgres) and the outer WHERE the second.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [constants] AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), [premium] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[tier] = @p0) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE [p].[active] = @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 bit', @p0 = N'gold', @p1 = 1;

mssql.raw: WITH [constants] AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), [premium] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[tier] = gold) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE [p].[active] = true;

prepared.sql: WITH `constants` AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), `premium` AS (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`tier` = ?) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE `p`.`active` = ?;

mysql.raw: WITH `constants` AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), `premium` AS (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`tier` = gold) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE `p`.`active` = true;

prepared.sql: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), "premium" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."tier" = $1) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE "p"."active" = $2;

postgres.raw: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), "premium" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."tier" = gold) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE "p"."active" = true;

prepared.sql: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), "premium" AS (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."tier" = ?) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE "p"."active" = ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH "constants" AS (SELECT 1 AS tier), "premium" AS (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."tier" = gold) SELECT * FROM premium AS p WHERE "p"."active" = true;

cases.name: cte referenced with fromTable is schema qualified
cases.note: A trap, pinned deliberately. `fromTable` always applies the dialect's default owner, so referencing a CTE that way emits `[dbo].[active_users]` on MSSQL and "public"."active_users" on Postgres — which does not resolve to the CTE. MySQL and SQLite have no default owner and come out fine. The dialect-neutral way to reference a CTE is fromRaw. A port must reproduce the qualification, not silently special-case CTE names.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0) SELECT * FROM [dbo].[active_users] AS [au] WHERE [au].[id] > @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'active', @p1 = 0;

mssql.raw: WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active) SELECT * FROM [dbo].[active_users] AS [au] WHERE [au].[id] > 0;

prepared.sql: WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = ?) SELECT * FROM `active_users` AS `au` WHERE `au`.`id` > ?;

mysql.raw: WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = active) SELECT * FROM `active_users` AS `au` WHERE `au`.`id` > 0;

prepared.sql: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = $1) SELECT * FROM "public"."active_users" AS "au" WHERE "au"."id" > $2;

postgres.raw: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active) SELECT * FROM "public"."active_users" AS "au" WHERE "au"."id" > 0;

prepared.sql: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = ?) SELECT * FROM "active_users" AS "au" WHERE "au"."id" > ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active) SELECT * FROM "active_users" AS "au" WHERE "au"."id" > 0;

cases.name: cte with no from clause is refused
cases.note: Errors inside a CTE body propagate out of the outer parse — the CTE is walked as a full statement, not as opaque text.

mssql.throws: From: No tables to select from

mysql.throws: From: No tables to select from

postgres.throws: From: No tables to select from

sqlite.throws: From: No tables to select from

cases.name: mssql top is emitted inside a union branch but dropped inside a cte body
cases.note: An asymmetry in the MSSQL TOP hook, frozen as-is. The union parser threads the dialect's `beforeSelectColumns` hook into each branch, so an explicit `.top(3)` on a union branch renders `SELECT TOP (3)`. The CTE parser does NOT thread the hook, so the identical `.top(5)` on the CTE body renders nothing at all — the golden's CTE is a plain `SELECT [u].[id]`. Being an inner statement is not the reason: the hook never looks at `isInnerStatement`, which is exactly why the union branch keeps its TOP despite being one. Reaching the hook is the whole difference. A Dart port that threads the hook uniformly through both emits an extra TOP in the CTE and diverges. Since 7.0.0 removed the automatic cap, every TOP in this statement is one the caller wrote; the golden has exactly one, on the union branch.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [capped] AS (SELECT TOP (5) [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]) SELECT [c].[id] FROM capped AS c WHERE [c].[id] > @p0 UNION ALL SELECT TOP (3) [a].[id] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a];', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 0;

mssql.raw: WITH [capped] AS (SELECT TOP (5) [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]) SELECT [c].[id] FROM capped AS c WHERE [c].[id] > 0 UNION ALL SELECT TOP (3) [a].[id] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a];

cases.name: cte outer query and cte body both render without a top
cases.note: Frozen mainly for what is NOT here. Until 7.0.0 MSSQL's unbounded-query guard looked only at the OUTER statement — no WHERE, no LIMIT, not an inner statement — so `TOP (1000)` landed on this outer SELECT even though the real row source is the CTE, whose own WHERE could never reach the guard. 7.0.0 removed the cap: the golden now has no TOP anywhere, and a port that reintroduces one fails here. What the case still pins on its own: the CTE name is quoted in the WITH clause (`WITH [active_users] AS`) while the fromRaw reference to it is emitted verbatim and unquoted (`FROM active_users AS au`) — which is why fromRaw is the dialect-neutral way to reach a CTE, and 'cte referenced with fromTable is schema qualified' is the same query through fromTable, where the reference gets schema-qualified into something that does not resolve. The CTE body is an inner statement, so the statement's only `;` is the outer one.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'active';

mssql.raw: WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au;

cases.name: union of two selects each with a bound value
cases.note: Set-operation branches bind AFTER the outer query's own WHERE values (the union clause is walked after WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING), so the params come out 'active' then 'pending' — outer first — and MSSQL numbers them `@p0`/`@p1` in that same order. Each branch is rendered as an inner statement, so no branch carries a trailing semicolon and the statement has exactly one, at the end. (Before 7.0.0 the inner-statement flag also kept the MSSQL TOP and the MySQL/Postgres/SQLite LIMIT safety nets out of each branch. There are no safety nets now, so the semicolon and the bind order are the whole test.)

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[name] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0 UNION SELECT [a].[name] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a] WHERE [a].[status] = @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'active', @p1 = N'pending';

mssql.raw: SELECT [u].[name] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active UNION SELECT [a].[name] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a] WHERE [a].[status] = pending;

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`name` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = ? UNION SELECT `a`.`name` FROM `admins` AS `a` WHERE `a`.`status` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`name` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = active UNION SELECT `a`.`name` FROM `admins` AS `a` WHERE `a`.`status` = pending;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = $1 UNION SELECT "a"."name" FROM "public"."admins" AS "a" WHERE "a"."status" = $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active UNION SELECT "a"."name" FROM "public"."admins" AS "a" WHERE "a"."status" = pending;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = ? UNION SELECT "a"."name" FROM "admins" AS "a" WHERE "a"."status" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active UNION SELECT "a"."name" FROM "admins" AS "a" WHERE "a"."status" = pending;

cases.name: union all intersect and except chained in one query
cases.note: Set operations share one ordered list and are emitted in the order they were added, single-space separated, with no parentheses around any branch. Values bind branch by branch, left to right. INTERSECT and EXCEPT are emitted for MySQL too, which only learned them in 8.0.31 — the builder does not gate on that.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 UNION ALL SELECT [a].[id] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a] INTERSECT SELECT [p].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[premium] AS [p] WHERE [p].[tier] = @p1 EXCEPT SELECT [b].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[banned] AS [b] WHERE [b].[reason] <> @p2;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'gold', @p2 = N'spam';

mssql.raw: SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true UNION ALL SELECT [a].[id] FROM [dbo].[admins] AS [a] INTERSECT SELECT [p].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[premium] AS [p] WHERE [p].[tier] = gold EXCEPT SELECT [b].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[banned] AS [b] WHERE [b].[reason] <> spam;

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`id` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? UNION ALL SELECT `a`.`id` FROM `admins` AS `a` INTERSECT SELECT `p`.`user_id` FROM `premium` AS `p` WHERE `p`.`tier` = ? EXCEPT SELECT `b`.`user_id` FROM `banned` AS `b` WHERE `b`.`reason` <> ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT `u`.`id` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true UNION ALL SELECT `a`.`id` FROM `admins` AS `a` INTERSECT SELECT `p`.`user_id` FROM `premium` AS `p` WHERE `p`.`tier` = gold EXCEPT SELECT `b`.`user_id` FROM `banned` AS `b` WHERE `b`.`reason` <> spam;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."id" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 UNION ALL SELECT "a"."id" FROM "public"."admins" AS "a" INTERSECT SELECT "p"."user_id" FROM "public"."premium" AS "p" WHERE "p"."tier" = $2 EXCEPT SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "public"."banned" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" <> $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."id" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true UNION ALL SELECT "a"."id" FROM "public"."admins" AS "a" INTERSECT SELECT "p"."user_id" FROM "public"."premium" AS "p" WHERE "p"."tier" = gold EXCEPT SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "public"."banned" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" <> spam;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."id" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ? UNION ALL SELECT "a"."id" FROM "admins" AS "a" INTERSECT SELECT "p"."user_id" FROM "premium" AS "p" WHERE "p"."tier" = ? EXCEPT SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "banned" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" <> ?;

cases.name: union with order by and limit on the whole result
cases.note: ORDER BY and LIMIT are emitted after the last set-operation branch, so they apply to the combined result rather than to the final branch. Watch the dialect split: MySQL/Postgres/SQLite render `LIMIT 10`, MSSQL renders `OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY`.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[name] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[type] = @p0 UNION SELECT [u].[name] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[type] = @p1 ORDER BY [u].[name] DESC OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'A', @p1 = N'B';

prepared.sql: SELECT `u`.`name` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`type` = ? UNION SELECT `u`.`name` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`type` = ? ORDER BY `u`.`name` DESC LIMIT 10;

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = $1 UNION SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = $2 ORDER BY "u"."name" DESC LIMIT 10;

postgres.raw: SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = A UNION SELECT "u"."name" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = B ORDER BY "u"."name" DESC LIMIT 10;

cases.note: Validation runs inside set-operation branches too — the branch is parsed as a full statement, so its errors surface from the outer parse.

cases.name: multi builder batch of insert update and select
cases.note: The whole point of a batch: placeholder numbering RESTARTS at each statement, because each is prepared independently (Postgres emits $1,$2 then $1,$2 then $1 — not $1..$5; MSSQL emits a fresh `SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql` with @p0 per statement). Statements are concatenated with no separator beyond each one's own trailing `;`. Note also the raw rendering ends with `COMMIT; ` — a trailing space the prepared rendering does not have.

prepared.sql: BEGIN TRANSACTION; SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [email]) VALUES (@p0, @p1);', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'John', @p1 = N'john@example.com';SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [s] SET [user_count] = @p0 FROM [dbo].[stats] AS [s] WHERE [s].[id] = @p1;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint', @p0 = 100, @p1 = 1;SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[email] = @p0;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'john@example.com';COMMIT TRANSACTION;

mssql.raw: BEGIN TRANSACTION; INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [email]) VALUES (John, john@example.com);UPDATE [s] SET [user_count] = 100 FROM [dbo].[stats] AS [s] WHERE [s].[id] = 1;SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[email] = john@example.com;COMMIT TRANSACTION;

prepared.sql: START TRANSACTION; INSERT INTO `users` (`name`, `email`) VALUES (?, ?);UPDATE `stats` AS `s` SET `user_count` = ? WHERE `s`.`id` = ?;SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`email` = ?;COMMIT;

mysql.raw: START TRANSACTION; INSERT INTO `users` (`name`, `email`) VALUES (John, john@example.com);UPDATE `stats` AS `s` SET `user_count` = 100 WHERE `s`.`id` = 1;SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`email` = john@example.com;COMMIT;

prepared.sql: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("name", "email") VALUES ($1, $2);UPDATE "public"."stats" AS "s" SET "user_count" = $1 WHERE "s"."id" = $2;SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."email" = $1;COMMIT;

postgres.raw: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("name", "email") VALUES (John, john@example.com);UPDATE "public"."stats" AS "s" SET "user_count" = 100 WHERE "s"."id" = 1;SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."email" = john@example.com;COMMIT;

prepared.sql: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "users" ("name", "email") VALUES (?, ?);UPDATE "stats" AS "s" SET "user_count" = ? WHERE "s"."id" = ?;SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."email" = ?;COMMIT;

sqlite.raw: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "users" ("name", "email") VALUES (John, john@example.com);UPDATE "stats" AS "s" SET "user_count" = 100 WHERE "s"."id" = 1;SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."email" = john@example.com;COMMIT;

cases.name: multi builder batch with transaction off
cases.note: With the transaction off, the batch is the bare concatenation of statements — no BEGIN/COMMIT and, importantly, no leading space either. With it on, the four dialects disagree on the delimiters: BEGIN TRANSACTION/COMMIT TRANSACTION (MSSQL), START TRANSACTION/COMMIT (MySQL), BEGIN/COMMIT (Postgres and SQLite).

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'DELETE [s] FROM [dbo].[sessions] AS [s] WHERE [s].[expired] = @p0;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'INSERT INTO [dbo].[sessions] ([user_id], [token]) VALUES (@p0, @p1);', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'abc123';

mssql.raw: DELETE [s] FROM [dbo].[sessions] AS [s] WHERE [s].[expired] = true;INSERT INTO [dbo].[sessions] ([user_id], [token]) VALUES (1, abc123);

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM `sessions` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`expired` = ?;INSERT INTO `sessions` (`user_id`, `token`) VALUES (?, ?);

mysql.raw: DELETE FROM `sessions` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`expired` = true;INSERT INTO `sessions` (`user_id`, `token`) VALUES (1, abc123);

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "public"."sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."expired" = $1;INSERT INTO "public"."sessions" ("user_id", "token") VALUES ($1, $2);

postgres.raw: DELETE FROM "public"."sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."expired" = true;INSERT INTO "public"."sessions" ("user_id", "token") VALUES (1, abc123);

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."expired" = ?;INSERT INTO "sessions" ("user_id", "token") VALUES (?, ?);

sqlite.raw: DELETE FROM "sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."expired" = true;INSERT INTO "sessions" ("user_id", "token") VALUES (1, abc123);

cases.name: multi builder batch whose statements carry a cte and a union
cases.note: Composition check: a batch statement is a full query, so CTE-first value ordering and set-operation branches behave inside a batch exactly as they do standalone — and the per-statement placeholder counter restarts across them regardless of nesting depth.

prepared.sql: BEGIN TRANSACTION; SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = @p0) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE [au].[id] > @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'active', @p1 = 0;SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[email] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[type] = @p0 UNION SELECT [s].[email] FROM [dbo].[subscribers] AS [s] WHERE [s].[confirmed] = @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 bit', @p0 = N'customer', @p1 = 1;COMMIT TRANSACTION;

mssql.raw: BEGIN TRANSACTION; WITH [active_users] AS (SELECT [u].[id] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[status] = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE [au].[id] > 0;SELECT [u].[email] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[type] = customer UNION SELECT [s].[email] FROM [dbo].[subscribers] AS [s] WHERE [s].[confirmed] = true;COMMIT TRANSACTION;

prepared.sql: START TRANSACTION; WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT `u`.`id` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = ?) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE `au`.`id` > ?;SELECT `u`.`email` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`type` = ? UNION SELECT `s`.`email` FROM `subscribers` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`confirmed` = ?;COMMIT;

mysql.raw: START TRANSACTION; WITH `active_users` AS (SELECT `u`.`id` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`status` = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE `au`.`id` > 0;SELECT `u`.`email` FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`type` = customer UNION SELECT `s`.`email` FROM `subscribers` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`confirmed` = true;COMMIT;

prepared.sql: BEGIN; WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT "u"."id" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = $1) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > $2;SELECT "u"."email" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = $1 UNION SELECT "s"."email" FROM "public"."subscribers" AS "s" WHERE "s"."confirmed" = $2;COMMIT;

postgres.raw: BEGIN; WITH "active_users" AS (SELECT "u"."id" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."status" = active) SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > 0;SELECT "u"."email" FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = customer UNION SELECT "s"."email" FROM "public"."subscribers" AS "s" WHERE "s"."confirmed" = true;COMMIT;

SELECT * FROM active_users AS au WHERE "au"."id" > ?;SELECT "u"."email" FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."type" = ? UNION SELECT "s"."email" FROM "subscribers" AS "s" WHERE "s"."confirmed" = ?;COMMIT;

cases.name: null is bound as a parameter and never dropped
cases.note: A null must occupy a placeholder, not vanish. If an implementation filters null out of the bound-value list (a very natural `where((v) => v != null)` in Dart), the SQL still emits three placeholders but only two values arrive: 'after' silently binds to the null's slot and every later parameter shifts by one. The query then runs, returns wrong rows, and nothing errors.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[a] IS NULL AND [u].[b] = @p0 AND [u].[c] = @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'after', @p1 = 7;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[a] IS NULL AND [u].[b] = after AND [u].[c] = 7;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`a` IS NULL AND `u`.`b` = ? AND `u`.`c` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`a` IS NULL AND `u`.`b` = after AND `u`.`c` = 7;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" IS NULL AND "u"."b" = $1 AND "u"."c" = $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" IS NULL AND "u"."b" = after AND "u"."c" = 7;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" IS NULL AND "u"."b" = ? AND "u"."c" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" IS NULL AND "u"."b" = after AND "u"."c" = 7;

cases.name: zero empty string and false are values not absences
cases.note: The three falsy traps in one query. A truthiness check anywhere on the value path (`if (value)`, `if (value.isNotEmpty)`, `value ?? fallback`) drops 0, '' or false, and each drop shifts every later parameter. The empty string is ALSO the TypeScript API's sentinel for 'no alias' — that sentinel meaning applies to identifier slots only, NEVER to a bound value, so `= ''` must still emit a placeholder and bind an empty string.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[count] = @p0 AND [u].[name] = @p1 AND [u].[active] = @p2 AND [u].[tail] = @p3;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 bit, @p3 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 0, @p1 = N'', @p2 = 0, @p3 = N'sentinel';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[count] = 0 AND [u].[name] =  AND [u].[active] = false AND [u].[tail] = sentinel;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`count` = ? AND `u`.`name` = ? AND `u`.`active` = ? AND `u`.`tail` = ?;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."count" = 0 AND "u"."name" =  AND "u"."active" = false AND "u"."tail" = sentinel;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."count" = ? AND "u"."name" = ? AND "u"."active" = ? AND "u"."tail" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."count" = 0 AND "u"."name" =  AND "u"."active" = false AND "u"."tail" = sentinel;

cases.name: string value containing a single quote
cases.note: The value is BOUND in MySQL/Postgres/SQLite, so none of them escapes it. MSSQL is the exception: it inlines values into the sp_executesql value list and must double the quote (N'O''Brien'). Miss the doubling and MSSQL emits a broken batch; add doubling anywhere else and the other three store a literal doubled quote in the row.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[name] = @p0;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'O''Brien';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[name] = O'Brien;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" = O'Brien;

cases.name: string value containing a backslash newline and tab
cases.note: A backslash is NOT an escape character on this path — the value is bound, so it must reach the driver byte-for-byte. MySQL's own literal syntax does treat \ as an escape, which tempts a port into pre-escaping it; do not. The newline and tab must survive as raw 0x0A/0x09, not as the two-character sequences \n and \t.

prepared.sql:
SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[path] = @p0;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'C:\Users\ada
line2	tabbed';

mssql.raw:
SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[path] = C:\Users\ada
line2	tabbed;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."path" = ?;

cases.name: string value containing unicode and emoji
cases.note: Pins that nothing on the value path measures, slices or re-encodes a string. Dart and JS both hold strings as UTF-16, so the emoji (a surrogate pair) and the ZWJ family sequence occupy the same code units in both — a port that converts to runes/code points, or truncates by length, diverges here.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[bio] = @p0;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'héllo wörld 👋 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 日本語 Ω';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[bio] = héllo wörld 👋 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 日本語 Ω;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`bio` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`bio` = héllo wörld 👋 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 日本語 Ω;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."bio" = $1;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."bio" = héllo wörld 👋 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 日本語 Ω;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."bio" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."bio" = héllo wörld 👋 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 日本語 Ω;

cases.name: sql injection payload is bound never interpolated
cases.note: The canary. The payload must appear ONLY as a bound parameter — or, for MSSQL, quote-doubled inside the sp_executesql value list — never as executable text in the statement body. If a port ever concatenates a value into the SQL, this case is what catches it.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[name] = @p0;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'''; DROP TABLE users; --';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[name] = '; DROP TABLE users; --;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`name` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`name` = '; DROP TABLE users; --;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" = $1;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" = '; DROP TABLE users; --;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" = '; DROP TABLE users; --;

cases.name: like wildcards live in the bound value not in the sql
cases.note: The % and _ belong to the value, not the operator — the builder never adds or strips them. A port that helpfully wraps the value in %...% itself would double them here.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[name] LIKE @p0 AND [u].[email] NOT LIKE @p1;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'%o''br_en%', @p1 = N'%@example.com';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[name] LIKE %o'br_en% AND [u].[email] NOT LIKE %@example.com;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`name` LIKE ? AND `u`.`email` NOT LIKE ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`name` LIKE %o'br_en% AND `u`.`email` NOT LIKE %@example.com;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" LIKE $1 AND "u"."email" NOT LIKE $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" LIKE %o'br_en% AND "u"."email" NOT LIKE %@example.com;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" LIKE ? AND "u"."email" NOT LIKE ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."name" LIKE %o'br_en% AND "u"."email" NOT LIKE %@example.com;

cases.name: a value that looks like a placeholder is bound not renumbered
cases.note: The mirror image of the raw-fragment placeholder tests: here the ?/$1/@p0 text arrives as a VALUE. Values never pass through the placeholder-substitution scan, so they must survive untouched and must not perturb Postgres's numbering — $2 still binds the second value, not the string '$1'.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[a] = @p0 AND [u].[b] = @p1 AND [u].[c] = @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'$1', @p1 = N'?', @p2 = N'@p0';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[a] = $1 AND [u].[b] = ? AND [u].[c] = @p0;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`a` = ? AND `u`.`b` = ? AND `u`.`c` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`a` = $1 AND `u`.`b` = ? AND `u`.`c` = @p0;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = $1 AND "u"."b" = $2 AND "u"."c" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = $1 AND "u"."b" = ? AND "u"."c" = @p0;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = ? AND "u"."b" = ? AND "u"."c" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = $1 AND "u"."b" = ? AND "u"."c" = @p0;

cases.name: datetime values render as utc iso8601 with exactly three fractional digits
cases.note: The biggest Dart landmine in the corpus. JS `Date.toISOString()` ALWAYS yields UTC, exactly 3 fractional digits, trailing Z. Dart's `DateTime.toIso8601String()` does neither: on a LOCAL DateTime it omits the Z and does NOT convert (a device at UTC+2 silently shifts the value two hours), and it emits 6 fractional digits whenever microseconds are non-zero. A Dart port MUST call .toUtc() and truncate to millisecond precision before rendering. The three values pin zero-ms, non-zero-ms, and the epoch boundary.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[events] AS [e] WHERE [e].[at] = @p0 AND [e].[until] < @p1 AND [e].[epoch] >= @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = '2024-01-15T12:00:00.000Z', @p1 = '2024-03-01T23:59:59.999Z', @p2 = '1970-01-01T00:00:00.000Z';
SELECT * FROM `events` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`at` = ? AND `e`.`until` < ? AND `e`.`epoch` >= ?;
SELECT * FROM "public"."events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."at" = $1 AND "e"."until" < $2 AND "e"."epoch" >= $3;
SELECT * FROM "events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."at" = ? AND "e"."until" < ? AND "e"."epoch" >= ?;

cases.name: negative integers are smallint never tinyint
cases.note: T-SQL `tinyint` is UNSIGNED 0-255. Declaring a negative as tinyint makes SQL Server raise an arithmetic-overflow error on every row. The band test must be `value >= 0 && value <= 255`, NOT the signed -128..127 byte range a port is likely to reach for from habit — that exact mistake is the bug this guards against.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[a] = @p0 AND [m].[b] = @p1 AND [m].[c] = @p2;', N'@p0 smallint, @p1 smallint, @p2 smallint', @p0 = -1, @p1 = -128, @p2 = -32768;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[a] = -1 AND [m].[b] = -128 AND [m].[c] = -32768;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`a` = ? AND `m`.`b` = ? AND `m`.`c` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`a` = -1 AND `m`.`b` = -128 AND `m`.`c` = -32768;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = $1 AND "m"."b" = $2 AND "m"."c" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = -1 AND "m"."b" = -128 AND "m"."c" = -32768;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = ? AND "m"."b" = ? AND "m"."c" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = -1 AND "m"."b" = -128 AND "m"."c" = -32768;

cases.name: tinyint boundary at 0 and 255 and 256
cases.note: The inclusive edges of the MSSQL tinyint band: 0 and 255 are tinyint, 256 spills to smallint. An off-by-one in either comparison shows up here and nowhere else.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[zero] = @p0 AND [m].[max_tinyint] = @p1 AND [m].[over] = @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 smallint', @p0 = 0, @p1 = 255, @p2 = 256;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[zero] = 0 AND [m].[max_tinyint] = 255 AND [m].[over] = 256;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`zero` = ? AND `m`.`max_tinyint` = ? AND `m`.`over` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`zero` = 0 AND `m`.`max_tinyint` = 255 AND `m`.`over` = 256;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."zero" = $1 AND "m"."max_tinyint" = $2 AND "m"."over" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."zero" = 0 AND "m"."max_tinyint" = 255 AND "m"."over" = 256;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."zero" = ? AND "m"."max_tinyint" = ? AND "m"."over" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."zero" = 0 AND "m"."max_tinyint" = 255 AND "m"."over" = 256;

cases.name: smallint int and bigint bands
cases.note: Every remaining MSSQL integer-band edge in one query: 32767/32768 (smallint to int), 2147483647/2147483648 (int to bigint), and the signed 32-bit floor. Only MSSQL renders these differently; the other three just bind the number.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[a] = @p0 AND [m].[b] = @p1 AND [m].[c] = @p2 AND [m].[d] = @p3 AND [m].[e] = @p4;', N'@p0 smallint, @p1 int, @p2 int, @p3 bigint, @p4 int', @p0 = 32767, @p1 = 32768, @p2 = 2147483647, @p3 = 2147483648, @p4 = -2147483648;
SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`a` = ? AND `m`.`b` = ? AND `m`.`c` = ? AND `m`.`d` = ? AND `m`.`e` = ?;
SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = $1 AND "m"."b" = $2 AND "m"."c" = $3 AND "m"."d" = $4 AND "m"."e" = $5;
SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = ? AND "m"."b" = ? AND "m"."c" = ? AND "m"."d" = ? AND "m"."e" = ?;

cases.name: integers beyond 2 to the 53 are float not bigint
cases.note: Past 2^53 an integer is not exactly representable as a double, so MSSQL declares it `float`, NOT `bigint`. The reason is textual: 1e21 renders via toString() as `1e+21`, which is not a legal bigint literal and made SQL Server reject the whole batch — float's literal syntax does accept scientific notation. Dart's int is a true 64-bit integer on the VM, so 9007199254740992 is an exact int there and a port will be tempted to call it bigint. It must apply the same is-safe-integer test JS does, AND reproduce JS's toString() output exactly (note `1e+21`, not 1000000000000000000000).

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[two53] = @p0 AND [m].[huge] = @p1;', N'@p0 float, @p1 float', @p0 = 9007199254740992, @p1 = 1e+21;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[two53] = 9007199254740992 AND [m].[huge] = 1e+21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`two53` = ? AND `m`.`huge` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`two53` = 9007199254740992 AND `m`.`huge` = 1e+21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."two53" = $1 AND "m"."huge" = $2;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."two53" = 9007199254740992 AND "m"."huge" = 1e+21;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."two53" = ? AND "m"."huge" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."two53" = 9007199254740992 AND "m"."huge" = 1e+21;

cases.name: mixed value types pin parameter order across clauses
cases.note: Parameters bind in EMISSION order — CTE, JOIN, WHERE, HAVING — which is not the order the builder methods were called. LIMIT and OFFSET are inlined into the SQL text and bind nothing; a port that binds them appends two phantom parameters. The IN list also carries a null and a double, so a filtering or coercing IN path shows up here too.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [recent] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[status] = @p0) SELECT [u].[id], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] AND [o].[kind] = @p1 WHERE [u].[name] LIKE @p2 AND [u].[age] BETWEEN @p3 AND @p4 AND [u].[status] IN (@p5, @p6, @p7) AND [u].[seen_at] > @p8 GROUP BY [u].[id] HAVING [u].[id] > @p9 ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 bit, @p2 nvarchar(max), @p3 tinyint, @p4 tinyint, @p5 nvarchar(max), @p6 nvarchar(max), @p7 float, @p8 nvarchar(max), @p9 tinyint', @p0 = N'shipped', @p1 = 1, @p2 = N'%ada%', @p3 = 18, @p4 = 65, @p5 = N'a', @p6 = NULL, @p7 = 2.5, @p8 = '2024-06-01T00:00:00.000Z', @p9 = 1;

mssql.raw: WITH [recent] AS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[status] = shipped) SELECT [u].[id], COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] AND [o].[kind] = true WHERE [u].[name] LIKE %ada% AND [u].[age] BETWEEN 18 AND 65 AND [u].[status] IN (a, , 2.5) AND [u].[seen_at] > 2024-06-01T00:00:00.000Z GROUP BY [u].[id] HAVING [u].[id] > 1 ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;

prepared.sql: WITH `recent` AS (SELECT * FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`status` = ?) SELECT `u`.`id`, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` AND `o`.`kind` = ? WHERE `u`.`name` LIKE ? AND `u`.`age` BETWEEN ? AND ? AND `u`.`status` IN (?, ?, ?) AND `u`.`seen_at` > ? GROUP BY `u`.`id` HAVING `u`.`id` > ? ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

mysql.raw: WITH `recent` AS (SELECT * FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`status` = shipped) SELECT `u`.`id`, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` AND `o`.`kind` = true WHERE `u`.`name` LIKE %ada% AND `u`.`age` BETWEEN 18 AND 65 AND `u`.`status` IN (a, , 2.5) AND `u`.`seen_at` > 2024-06-01T00:00:00.000Z GROUP BY `u`.`id` HAVING `u`.`id` > 1 ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = $1) SELECT "u"."id", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."kind" = $2 WHERE "u"."name" LIKE $3 AND "u"."age" BETWEEN $4 AND $5 AND "u"."status" IN ($6, $7, $8) AND "u"."seen_at" > $9 GROUP BY "u"."id" HAVING "u"."id" > $10 ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

postgres.raw: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT * FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = shipped) SELECT "u"."id", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."kind" = true WHERE "u"."name" LIKE %ada% AND "u"."age" BETWEEN 18 AND 65 AND "u"."status" IN (a, , 2.5) AND "u"."seen_at" > 2024-06-01T00:00:00.000Z GROUP BY "u"."id" HAVING "u"."id" > 1 ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

prepared.sql: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT * FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = ?) SELECT "u"."id", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."kind" = ? WHERE "u"."name" LIKE ? AND "u"."age" BETWEEN ? AND ? AND "u"."status" IN (?, ?, ?) AND "u"."seen_at" > ? GROUP BY "u"."id" HAVING "u"."id" > ? ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

sqlite.raw: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT * FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."status" = shipped) SELECT "u"."id", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" AND "o"."kind" = true WHERE "u"."name" LIKE %ada% AND "u"."age" BETWEEN 18 AND 65 AND "u"."status" IN (a, , 2.5) AND "u"."seen_at" > 2024-06-01T00:00:00.000Z GROUP BY "u"."id" HAVING "u"."id" > 1 ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT 10  OFFSET 20;

cases.name: update binds set values before where values
cases.note: SET is emitted before WHERE, so the new value binds ahead of the row selector. Reverse them and the UPDATE writes the id into the name column of whichever row matched 'Ada'. The null SET value must also hold its slot.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [u] SET [name] = @p0, [age] = @p1 FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 tinyint', @p0 = N'Ada', @p1 = NULL, @p2 = 7;

mssql.raw: UPDATE [u] SET [name] = Ada, [age] =  FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] = 7;

prepared.sql: UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `name` = ?, `age` = ? WHERE `u`.`id` = ?;

cases.name: column name containing the mssql closing bracket is escaped
cases.note: Identifier names are user-controlled (they come from a dataset's schema), so they are an injection surface. MSSQL must double the `]` so the name cannot close the quoting and inject `OR [1=1`. The other three have nothing to escape here and pass the brackets through as ordinary characters — that asymmetry is the point: each dialect doubles ONLY its own closing delimiter.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[x]] OR [1=1] FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u];', N'';

cases.name: table name and alias containing a double quote are escaped
cases.note: The Postgres/SQLite breakout: an unescaped double quote terminates the identifier and turns the rest into SQL. Postgres and SQLite double it; MSSQL and MySQL emit it verbatim inside their own delimiters. The ALIAS is hostile too — aliases go through the same quoting path as names, and a port that quotes names but forgets aliases still passes the table half of this case while leaking through the alias.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[x" FROM secret--] AS [a"b];', N'';

cases.note: MySQL's breakout character. Doubling the backtick is what keeps a`b a single identifier; the other three treat it as an ordinary character. Table, column and column-alias slots are all exercised because they are separate call sites and a port can easily quote one and forget another.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [u].[a`b] AS [c`d] FROM [dbo].[t`bl] AS [u];', N'';

cases.name: nul byte in an identifier is refused
cases.note: Some drivers truncate a C string at the first NUL, so `bad�name` could reach the server as `bad` — silently addressing a different column. Refuse it at build time instead. Must throw in every dialect, before any SQL is produced.

mssql.throws: General: identifier contains a NUL byte

mysql.throws: General: identifier contains a NUL byte

postgres.throws: General: identifier contains a NUL byte

sqlite.throws: General: identifier contains a NUL byte

cases.name: nul byte in a raw fragment is refused
cases.note: Load-bearing for the entire placeholder scheme. The internal placeholder token is NUL-delimited, so a raw fragment carrying NUL could FORGE a token and steal a bound value's slot. Rejecting NUL in every caller-supplied fragment is precisely what makes the token unforgeable from caller text — a port that skips this check has a parameter-smuggling hole, not merely a cosmetic gap.

mssql.throws: General: SQL fragment contains a NUL byte

mysql.throws: General: SQL fragment contains a NUL byte

postgres.throws: General: SQL fragment contains a NUL byte

sqlite.throws: General: SQL fragment contains a NUL byte

cases.name: nul byte in a bound value diverges by dialect
cases.note: A deliberate divergence that must be reproduced exactly. A NUL inside a VALUE is not rejected: values bypass the fragment path that screens for NUL, so MySQL/Postgres/SQLite bind the string with its NUL intact and leave it to the driver. MSSQL differs because it inlines values into the sp_executesql text (to-sql.ts appends `@pN = <value>` via the fragment path), so there the NUL reaches the fragment check and the whole statement is refused. Do not 'harmonize' this by rejecting NUL values everywhere, and do not let MSSQL emit one.

mssql.throws: General: SQL fragment contains a NUL byte

cases.name: empty not in is refused
cases.note: `NOT IN ()` is a syntax error in every dialect, and an empty NOT IN almost always means the caller's filter logic produced nothing. It must throw with its OWN message (not the IN one) and must never be silently dropped — dropping it would WIDEN the result set instead of narrowing it, which is the dangerous direction to fail.

mssql.throws: Where: NOT IN requires at least one value

mysql.throws: Where: NOT IN requires at least one value

postgres.throws: Where: NOT IN requires at least one value

sqlite.throws: Where: NOT IN requires at least one value

cases.name: raw fragments are emitted verbatim and are a trust boundary
cases.note: Nothing in a raw fragment is escaped, quoted or parameterized: the semicolon, the -- comment and the block comment all survive intact (note that the resulting statement is nonsense — that is the demonstration, not a bug). This is the contract: raw is for SQL the CALLER controls, and it is the one place caller text becomes executable SQL, so never build one from user input. A port must not 'helpfully' sanitize these, or every legitimate raw fragment breaks.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

prepared.sql: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

mysql.raw: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

prepared.sql: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

postgres.raw: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

prepared.sql: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

sqlite.raw: SELECT 1 AS one; -- trailing comment FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE 1 = 1 /* still raw */;

cases.name: every clause contributing a value pins the full parameter order
cases.note: The single strongest ordering assertion in the corpus. to-sql walks CTE -> SELECT -> FROM -> JOIN -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> UNION -> ORDER BY, and each clause splices its sub-values in that order. Here seven values are bound from seven different clauses: the CTE body (10.5), a scalar subquery in the SELECT list ('click'), a derived table in FROM (true), a joinOn VALUE (100), the outer WHERE (0), the HAVING (999) and the UNION branch (2020). Expected order is exactly that. No authored case pins more than two clauses at once, so a port that, say, collects join values after where values, or hoists HAVING before GROUP BY, still emits byte-identical SQL on MySQL/SQLite and silently binds the wrong rows. Postgres is the only dialect where the mis-order is even visible ($n renumbering), and MSSQL's inlined sp_executesql values make it visible too.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [recent] AS (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > @p0) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[logs] AS [l] WHERE [l].[kind] = @p1) AS [click_count], [au].[id] FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p2) AS [au] INNER JOIN [dbo].[payments] AS [p] ON [au].[id] = [p].[user_id] AND [p].[amount] > @p3 WHERE [au].[id] > @p4 GROUP BY [au].[id] HAVING [au].[id] < @p5 UNION ALL SELECT [x].[id] FROM [dbo].[archive] AS [x] WHERE [x].[year] = @p6;', N'@p0 float, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 bit, @p3 tinyint, @p4 tinyint, @p5 smallint, @p6 smallint', @p0 = 10.5, @p1 = N'click', @p2 = 1, @p3 = 100, @p4 = 0, @p5 = 999, @p6 = 2020;

mssql.raw: WITH [recent] AS (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > 10.5) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [dbo].[logs] AS [l] WHERE [l].[kind] = click) AS [click_count], [au].[id] FROM (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true) AS [au] INNER JOIN [dbo].[payments] AS [p] ON [au].[id] = [p].[user_id] AND [p].[amount] > 100 WHERE [au].[id] > 0 GROUP BY [au].[id] HAVING [au].[id] < 999 UNION ALL SELECT [x].[id] FROM [dbo].[archive] AS [x] WHERE [x].[year] = 2020;

prepared.sql: WITH `recent` AS (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > ?) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `logs` AS `l` WHERE `l`.`kind` = ?) AS `click_count`, `au`.`id` FROM (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ?) AS `au` INNER JOIN `payments` AS `p` ON `au`.`id` = `p`.`user_id` AND `p`.`amount` > ? WHERE `au`.`id` > ? GROUP BY `au`.`id` HAVING `au`.`id` < ? UNION ALL SELECT `x`.`id` FROM `archive` AS `x` WHERE `x`.`year` = ?;

mysql.raw: WITH `recent` AS (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > 10.5) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `logs` AS `l` WHERE `l`.`kind` = click) AS `click_count`, `au`.`id` FROM (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true) AS `au` INNER JOIN `payments` AS `p` ON `au`.`id` = `p`.`user_id` AND `p`.`amount` > 100 WHERE `au`.`id` > 0 GROUP BY `au`.`id` HAVING `au`.`id` < 999 UNION ALL SELECT `x`.`id` FROM `archive` AS `x` WHERE `x`.`year` = 2020;

prepared.sql: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > $1) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."logs" AS "l" WHERE "l"."kind" = $2) AS "click_count", "au"."id" FROM (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $3) AS "au" INNER JOIN "public"."payments" AS "p" ON "au"."id" = "p"."user_id" AND "p"."amount" > $4 WHERE "au"."id" > $5 GROUP BY "au"."id" HAVING "au"."id" < $6 UNION ALL SELECT "x"."id" FROM "public"."archive" AS "x" WHERE "x"."year" = $7;

postgres.raw: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 10.5) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "public"."logs" AS "l" WHERE "l"."kind" = click) AS "click_count", "au"."id" FROM (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true) AS "au" INNER JOIN "public"."payments" AS "p" ON "au"."id" = "p"."user_id" AND "p"."amount" > 100 WHERE "au"."id" > 0 GROUP BY "au"."id" HAVING "au"."id" < 999 UNION ALL SELECT "x"."id" FROM "public"."archive" AS "x" WHERE "x"."year" = 2020;

prepared.sql: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > ?) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "logs" AS "l" WHERE "l"."kind" = ?) AS "click_count", "au"."id" FROM (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ?) AS "au" INNER JOIN "payments" AS "p" ON "au"."id" = "p"."user_id" AND "p"."amount" > ? WHERE "au"."id" > ? GROUP BY "au"."id" HAVING "au"."id" < ? UNION ALL SELECT "x"."id" FROM "archive" AS "x" WHERE "x"."year" = ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH "recent" AS (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 10.5) SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "logs" AS "l" WHERE "l"."kind" = click) AS "click_count", "au"."id" FROM (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true) AS "au" INNER JOIN "payments" AS "p" ON "au"."id" = "p"."user_id" AND "p"."amount" > 100 WHERE "au"."id" > 0 GROUP BY "au"."id" HAVING "au"."id" < 999 UNION ALL SELECT "x"."id" FROM "archive" AS "x" WHERE "x"."year" = 2020;

cases.name: a union branch that carries its own cte emits WITH inside the UNION
cases.note: defaultUnion renders each branch with the full defaultToSql, and that walk emits the branch's cteStates FIRST — so a CTE declared inside a union branch produces `... UNION ALL WITH "t" AS (...) SELECT ...`, which no engine accepts. Frozen contract: reproduce it, do not hoist the CTE to the head of the statement. Also pins the value order across the boundary: the OUTER where value binds before the branch's CTE value, which binds before the branch's own where value.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 UNION ALL WITH [banned] AS (SELECT [b].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[bans] AS [b] WHERE [b].[reason] = @p1) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE [bn].[user_id] > @p2;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'spam', @p2 = 0;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true UNION ALL WITH [banned] AS (SELECT [b].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[bans] AS [b] WHERE [b].[reason] = spam) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE [bn].[user_id] > 0;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? UNION ALL WITH `banned` AS (SELECT `b`.`user_id` FROM `bans` AS `b` WHERE `b`.`reason` = ?) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE `bn`.`user_id` > ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true UNION ALL WITH `banned` AS (SELECT `b`.`user_id` FROM `bans` AS `b` WHERE `b`.`reason` = spam) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE `bn`.`user_id` > 0;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 UNION ALL WITH "banned" AS (SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "public"."bans" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" = $2) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE "bn"."user_id" > $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true UNION ALL WITH "banned" AS (SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "public"."bans" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" = spam) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE "bn"."user_id" > 0;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ? UNION ALL WITH "banned" AS (SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "bans" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" = ?) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE "bn"."user_id" > ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true UNION ALL WITH "banned" AS (SELECT "b"."user_id" FROM "bans" AS "b" WHERE "b"."reason" = spam) SELECT * FROM banned AS bn WHERE "bn"."user_id" > 0;

cases.name: an explicit top inside a derived table is silently dropped
cases.note: MSSQL's TOP is injected by the `beforeSelectColumns` hook in ToSqlOptions, and defaultFrom/defaultJoin/defaultSelect/defaultWhere all call `defaultToSql(sub, config, mode)` WITHOUT passing options. So `.top(5)` inside a fromWithBuilder produces NO TOP at all — the row cap the caller explicitly asked for vanishes. Compare with the next case, where the union parser DOES thread options through and the same op does render. Since 7.0.0 the MSSQL golden contains no TOP whatsoever and is byte-identical to 'from derived table', which is this op-list minus the top: the pin is now the strong one, that `.top(5)` here changes nothing at all. (Before 7.0.0 the outer SELECT still collected the automatic `TOP (1000)`, so the pin was the weaker 'exactly one TOP, and it is not the one the caller wrote'.) A Dart port that threads its options object uniformly through every subquery emits `TOP (5)` in the derived table and fails.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM (SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]) AS [sub];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u]) AS [sub];

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u`) AS `sub`;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u`) AS `sub`;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u") AS "sub";

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u") AS "sub";

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u") AS "sub";

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u") AS "sub";

cases.name: an explicit top inside a union branch is emitted
cases.note: The mirror image of the previous case. defaultUnion is the ONLY sub-parser that forwards `options` into defaultToSql, so `.top(5)` inside a union branch DOES render `SELECT TOP (5)` on MSSQL — even though the branch is an inner statement, because the hook never tests `isInnerStatement`. The MSSQL golden has exactly one TOP and it is the branch's explicit one; since 7.0.0 removed the automatic cap that is the only kind of TOP there is, and the outer query's WHERE has nothing to do with it. Together with the previous case this pins an inconsistency a port will almost certainly normalize away in one direction or the other.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 UNION ALL SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[archive] AS [a];', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true UNION ALL SELECT TOP (5) * FROM [dbo].[archive] AS [a];

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? UNION ALL SELECT * FROM `archive` AS `a`;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true UNION ALL SELECT * FROM `archive` AS `a`;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 UNION ALL SELECT * FROM "public"."archive" AS "a";

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true UNION ALL SELECT * FROM "public"."archive" AS "a";

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true UNION ALL SELECT * FROM "archive" AS "a";

cases.name: mssql top combined with joins and an order by
cases.note: TOP is emitted by the select hook, so it lands before the column list and BEFORE the JOIN clauses — `SELECT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN ...`. Note also that `.top(n)` and an ORDER BY coexist without complaint: top is not pagination, and with no limit and no offset set, to-sql never calls defaultLimitOffset at all, so neither the TOP/LIMIT conflict nor the ORDER BY guards are reached. The three other dialects ignore top entirely and put no LIMIT in its place — their goldens are the plain joined SELECT. (Before 7.0.0 this was the contract in the opposite direction: the explicit top suppressed MSSQL's automatic cap, and the note called it out that an unbounded MySQL/Postgres/SQLite SELECT was genuinely unbounded while the MSSQL one was capped. That asymmetry is gone; all four are unbounded unless the caller says otherwise.)

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC;', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT TOP (10) * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] INNER JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` INNER JOIN `orders` AS `o` ON `u`.`id` = `o`.`user_id` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "public"."orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" INNER JOIN "orders" AS "o" ON "u"."id" = "o"."user_id" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC;

cases.name: top zero is absent for rendering but present for the limit conflict check
cases.note: Two different tests read customState['top']. The select hook requires `top > 0` to render, so top(0) renders nothing. The MSSQL limit/offset guard only tests presence (`!== null && !== undefined`), so top(0) DOES trip 'MSSQL should not use both TOP and LIMIT/OFFSET in the same query'. MSSQL therefore THROWS here while MySQL/Postgres/SQLite ignore the top and happily emit LIMIT 10. A Dart port modelling top as `int?` and treating 0 as unset never throws; one that treats it as set renders `TOP (0)`. Both fail.

mssql.throws: LimitOffset: MSSQL should not use both TOP and LIMIT/OFFSET in the same query

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" LIMIT 10;
