cases.note: limit(0) and negative limits throw ParserError (M1).

mssql.throws: LimitOffset: LIMIT must be a positive integer

mysql.throws: LimitOffset: LIMIT must be a positive integer

postgres.throws: LimitOffset: LIMIT must be a positive integer

sqlite.throws: LimitOffset: LIMIT must be a positive integer

cases.name: offset without a limit on an unfiltered query emits no cap
cases.note: The case that moved most in 7.0.0. In 6.x this was the ONLY route to the MySQL/Postgres/SQLite automatic LIMIT: defaultLimitOffset ran (offset > 0), saw limit === 0, no inner statement and no WHERE, and emitted `LIMIT 1000 OFFSET 5`; MSSQL reached the same cap down its own branch and spelled it `FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY`, with no TOP, since TOP and OFFSET cannot share a SELECT (Msg 10741). A caller paging an unfiltered table got 1000 rows and no sign the rest existed. 7.0.0 removed the cap: Postgres now emits a bare `  OFFSET 5` and returns every row past the fifth, MySQL and SQLite emit only their sentinel limits — which bound nothing — and MSSQL emits `OFFSET 5 ROWS` with no FETCH. The golden is byte-identical to 'offset with a where and no limit: only postgres keeps the doubled space' apart from the WHERE, because the WHERE no longer touches the tail; a port whose two goldens differ has kept a cap somewhere.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 5 ROWS;', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 5 ROWS;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 5;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 5;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 5;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 5;

cases.name: offset with a where and no limit: only postgres keeps the doubled space
cases.note: SPACING, byte-for-byte. to-sql prefixes the limit/offset helper with " ", and the helper's offset branch starts with ANOTHER leading space (" OFFSET "). Postgres is the one dialect that accepts a standalone OFFSET and therefore emits nothing between the two spaces, so the result is `... ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 5;` with TWO spaces. A port that normalizes whitespace, or builds the clause list by joining on " ", collapses it and fails. MySQL and SQLite fill that gap with their sentinel limit (`LIMIT 18446744073709551615` / `LIMIT -1`) and so emit a single space; MSSQL's branch has no leading space and also emits one. The doubled space long predates 7.0.0 and is not a symptom of the removed cap — it is simply what an empty limit slot looks like. What 7.0.0 changed is how many queries can reach it: compare 'offset without a limit on an unfiltered query emits no cap', which is this case without the WHERE and now has the same two spaces, where 6.x filled the slot with `LIMIT 1000`.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0 ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 5 ROWS;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[active] = true ORDER BY [u].[id] ASC OFFSET 5 ROWS;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = ? ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 5;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`active` = true ORDER BY `u`.`id` ASC LIMIT 18446744073709551615 OFFSET 5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = $1 ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 5;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC  OFFSET 5;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = ? ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 5;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."active" = true ORDER BY "u"."id" ASC LIMIT -1 OFFSET 5;

cases.name: order by direction None renders DESC
cases.note: defaultOrderBy tests `direction === Ascending` and sends EVERYTHING ELSE to the else branch — so OrderByDirection.None, a public enum member, emits ` DESC`, not ` ASC` and not the dialect default. This silently reverses a caller's result set. A Dart port with an exhaustive switch on the enum either throws or emits nothing for None; either way it diverges. Pinned alongside an explicit Descending and a raw fragment so the ', ' separator between mixed entries is fixed too.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] ORDER BY [u].[id], [u].[name] DESC, LENGTH(u.email) ASC;', N'';

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" ORDER BY "u"."id", "u"."name" DESC, LENGTH(u.email) ASC;

cases.name: where with the None operator emits no comparison but still binds the value
cases.note: WhereOperator.None is a public enum member and the where parser's switch has no case for it, so the operator slot renders as the empty string between two spaces: `"u"."x"  ?`. The value is STILL bound (addDynamicValue runs unconditionally), so the params array is unaffected and only the SQL text is broken. A Dart port with an exhaustive `switch` on the enum will not compile without a None arm — whatever it puts there (throw, '=', '') must be the empty string to match.

cases.name: having and where render the same operator identically
cases.note: The same predicate expressed as WHERE and as HAVING must emit the same operator token. They diverged: WHERE handled LIKE, HAVING dropped it.

prepared.sql: SELECT "u"."role" AS "role", COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM "public"."users" AS "u" GROUP BY "u"."role" HAVING "u"."role" LIKE $1;

cases.name: onGroup binds the values inside the group before the outer where
cases.note: Pins parameter ORDER through a join-on group. The group value is emitted while walking the JOIN clause, which precedes WHERE, so it takes the first slot. Previously the group was discarded entirely and this value was never bound at all.

cases.name: cross join with no on conditions and a second join after it
cases.note: defaultJoinOns returns early when the ON list is empty, so NO ` ON ` token is emitted — `CROSS JOIN [dbo].[regions] AS [r]` stands alone. A port that always emits ` ON ` (or ` ON 1=1`) breaks. The second join also pins the inter-join separator: joins are joined by a single space, emitted only when `i < len - 1`, so there is no trailing space before WHERE.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] CROSS JOIN [dbo].[regions] AS [r] LEFT JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] WHERE [u].[active] = @p0;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] CROSS JOIN [dbo].[regions] AS [r] LEFT JOIN [dbo].[orders] AS [o] ON [u].[id] = [o].[user_id] WHERE [u].[active] = true;

cases.name: identifiers containing every dialect's closing delimiter and a quote
cases.note: quoteIdentifier doubles ONLY the dialect's own closing delimiter: `]`->`]]` on MSSQL, `"`->`""` on Postgres/SQLite, backtick->double-backtick on MySQL. The same name therefore escapes differently in each dialect and passes the other delimiters through untouched — one shared 'escape all the quote chars' helper in a port is wrong everywhere. The apostrophe adds a second layer: MSSQL wraps the finished statement in sp_executesql and doubles EVERY single quote in the SQL text, including the ones inside identifiers, so `al'ias` becomes `[al''ias]` there and stays `"al'ias"` elsewhere. The dotted alias pins that a `.` inside a name is quoted as one identifier, never split.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT [a`b].[c]]d"e''f] AS [al''ias] FROM [dbo].[we"ird]]tbl] AS [a`b] INNER JOIN [dbo].[plain] AS [p.q] ON [a`b].[id] = [p.q].[id] WHERE [a`b].[n]]m] = @p0;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'O''Brien';

mssql.raw: SELECT [a`b].[c]]d"e'f] AS [al'ias] FROM [dbo].[we"ird]]tbl] AS [a`b] INNER JOIN [dbo].[plain] AS [p.q] ON [a`b].[id] = [p.q].[id] WHERE [a`b].[n]]m] = O'Brien;

prepared.sql: SELECT `a``b`.`c]d"e'f` AS `al'ias` FROM `we"ird]tbl` AS `a``b` INNER JOIN `plain` AS `p.q` ON `a``b`.`id` = `p.q`.`id` WHERE `a``b`.`n]m` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT `a``b`.`c]d"e'f` AS `al'ias` FROM `we"ird]tbl` AS `a``b` INNER JOIN `plain` AS `p.q` ON `a``b`.`id` = `p.q`.`id` WHERE `a``b`.`n]m` = O'Brien;

prepared.sql: SELECT "a`b"."c]d""e'f" AS "al'ias" FROM "public"."we""ird]tbl" AS "a`b" INNER JOIN "public"."plain" AS "p.q" ON "a`b"."id" = "p.q"."id" WHERE "a`b"."n]m" = $1;

postgres.raw: SELECT "a`b"."c]d""e'f" AS "al'ias" FROM "public"."we""ird]tbl" AS "a`b" INNER JOIN "public"."plain" AS "p.q" ON "a`b"."id" = "p.q"."id" WHERE "a`b"."n]m" = O'Brien;

prepared.sql: SELECT "a`b"."c]d""e'f" AS "al'ias" FROM "we""ird]tbl" AS "a`b" INNER JOIN "plain" AS "p.q" ON "a`b"."id" = "p.q"."id" WHERE "a`b"."n]m" = ?;

cases.name: an integral double and an int render identically in typescript
cases.note: THE Dart divergence. JavaScript has one number type, so `{t:'double', v:2}` and `{t:'int', v:2}` are the same value: both inline as `2` in parseRaw and both declare `@pN tinyint` in MSSQL (Number.isSafeInteger(2.0) is true). Dart's `2.0` is a genuine double: `toString()` gives `2.0` and any type inference sees a double, so a Dart driver that faithfully constructs a double from the `double` tag will emit `2.0` / `float` and fail — while one that collapses both tags to `int` passes this case but fails the fractional ones. The correct Dart driver must construct a double AND render it through the same integral-collapsing path TypeScript uses. -3 is included so the tinyint/smallint boundary is crossed by both tags at once.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[a] = @p0 AND [m].[b] = @p1 AND [m].[c] = @p2 AND [m].[d] = @p3;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 smallint, @p3 smallint', @p0 = 2, @p1 = 2, @p2 = -3, @p3 = -3;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[a] = 2 AND [m].[b] = 2 AND [m].[c] = -3 AND [m].[d] = -3;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`a` = ? AND `m`.`b` = ? AND `m`.`c` = ? AND `m`.`d` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`a` = 2 AND `m`.`b` = 2 AND `m`.`c` = -3 AND `m`.`d` = -3;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = $1 AND "m"."b" = $2 AND "m"."c" = $3 AND "m"."d" = $4;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = 2 AND "m"."b" = 2 AND "m"."c" = -3 AND "m"."d" = -3;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = ? AND "m"."b" = ? AND "m"."c" = ? AND "m"."d" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."a" = 2 AND "m"."b" = 2 AND "m"."c" = -3 AND "m"."d" = -3;

cases.name: doubles that render in exponent notation
cases.note: Number rendering, the other half. 1e21 is an integer by `Number.isInteger` but NOT by `Number.isSafeInteger`, so MSSQL declares it `float` (deliberately — src/parser/to-sql.ts notes that `bigint` has no scientific-notation literal and SQL Server rejected the batch) and inlines it as `1e+21` via toString(). 1.5e-7 renders `1.5e-7`; 0.1 renders `0.1`. Dart's `double.toString()` agrees on all three ONLY if the port uses the shortest-round-trip form and does not reach for `toStringAsFixed`/`NumberFormat`. dart2js and the Dart VM must also agree — this is the case that catches them if they do not.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[huge] = @p0 AND [m].[tiny] = @p1 AND [m].[frac] = @p2;', N'@p0 float, @p1 float, @p2 float', @p0 = 1e+21, @p1 = 1.5e-7, @p2 = 0.1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[m] AS [m] WHERE [m].[huge] = 1e+21 AND [m].[tiny] = 1.5e-7 AND [m].[frac] = 0.1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`huge` = ? AND `m`.`tiny` = ? AND `m`.`frac` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `m` AS `m` WHERE `m`.`huge` = 1e+21 AND `m`.`tiny` = 1.5e-7 AND `m`.`frac` = 0.1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."huge" = $1 AND "m"."tiny" = $2 AND "m"."frac" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."huge" = 1e+21 AND "m"."tiny" = 1.5e-7 AND "m"."frac" = 0.1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."huge" = ? AND "m"."tiny" = ? AND "m"."frac" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "m" AS "m" WHERE "m"."huge" = 1e+21 AND "m"."tiny" = 1.5e-7 AND "m"."frac" = 0.1;

cases.name: datetime precision below a millisecond is truncated
cases.note: JavaScript's Date holds MILLISECONDS; Dart's DateTime holds MICROSECONDS. Given `...12:00:00.123456Z`, `new Date(...).toISOString()` yields `.123Z` — the extra digits are gone from both the bound param and MSSQL's inlined literal. Dart's `DateTime.parse` keeps them and `toIso8601String()` emits `.123456Z`, so the Dart driver MUST truncate to milliseconds at the boundary. Also pins a pre-epoch instant (negative epoch ms, a classic overflow/format trap) and a whereBetween of two datetimes, whose two values must bind low-then-high between the surrounding predicates.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[events] AS [e] WHERE [e].[precise_at] = @p0 AND [e].[occurred_at] BETWEEN @p1 AND @p2 AND [e].[tail] = @p3;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 nvarchar(max), @p3 tinyint', @p0 = '2024-01-15T12:00:00.123Z', @p1 = '1969-07-20T20:17:40.000Z', @p2 = '2024-01-15T12:00:00.500Z', @p3 = 1;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[events] AS [e] WHERE [e].[precise_at] = 2024-01-15T12:00:00.123Z AND [e].[occurred_at] BETWEEN 1969-07-20T20:17:40.000Z AND 2024-01-15T12:00:00.500Z AND [e].[tail] = 1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `events` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`precise_at` = ? AND `e`.`occurred_at` BETWEEN ? AND ? AND `e`.`tail` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `events` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`precise_at` = 2024-01-15T12:00:00.123Z AND `e`.`occurred_at` BETWEEN 1969-07-20T20:17:40.000Z AND 2024-01-15T12:00:00.500Z AND `e`.`tail` = 1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."precise_at" = $1 AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN $2 AND $3 AND "e"."tail" = $4;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."precise_at" = 2024-01-15T12:00:00.123Z AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN 1969-07-20T20:17:40.000Z AND 2024-01-15T12:00:00.500Z AND "e"."tail" = 1;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."precise_at" = ? AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN ? AND ? AND "e"."tail" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "events" AS "e" WHERE "e"."precise_at" = 2024-01-15T12:00:00.123Z AND "e"."occurred_at" BETWEEN 1969-07-20T20:17:40.000Z AND 2024-01-15T12:00:00.500Z AND "e"."tail" = 1;

cases.name: a subquery inside a subquery binds depth first
cases.note: INTERACTION. A whereIn subquery whose own WHERE contains a NOT EXISTS subquery — two levels of nesting below the outer statement. Each level is rendered by a fresh defaultToSql and its values are spliced into the parent at the position of its placeholder, so the order is 50 (inner-1 where), 5 (inner-2 where), then the outer 'x'. Both inner statements are marked isInnerStatement, so neither gets a trailing ';' — the whole statement has exactly one, at the very end. (Before 7.0.0 that flag also kept the MSSQL safety-net TOP out of each inner statement, and the outer query's WHERE kept it off the outer one. The cap is gone, so the semicolon and the bind order are what remains.)

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > @p0 AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[refunds] AS [r] WHERE [r].[amount] > @p1)) AND [u].[status] = @p2;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 50, @p1 = 5, @p2 = N'x';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] IN (SELECT [o].[user_id] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[total] > 50 AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[refunds] AS [r] WHERE [r].[amount] > 5)) AND [u].[status] = x;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > ? AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM `refunds` AS `r` WHERE `r`.`amount` > ?)) AND `u`.`status` = ?;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`id` IN (SELECT `o`.`user_id` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`total` > 50 AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM `refunds` AS `r` WHERE `r`.`amount` > 5)) AND `u`.`status` = x;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > $1 AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "public"."refunds" AS "r" WHERE "r"."amount" > $2)) AND "u"."status" = $3;

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 50 AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "public"."refunds" AS "r" WHERE "r"."amount" > 5)) AND "u"."status" = x;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > ? AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "refunds" AS "r" WHERE "r"."amount" > ?)) AND "u"."status" = ?;

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."id" IN (SELECT "o"."user_id" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."total" > 50 AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "refunds" AS "r" WHERE "r"."amount" > 5)) AND "u"."status" = x;

cases.name: a where group nested inside a where group
cases.note: INTERACTION. whereGroup pushes THREE states (GroupBegin, GroupBuilder-holding-a-child-state, GroupEnd) and the child's predicates are rendered by a recursive defaultWhere whose leading 'WHERE ' prefix is then stripped. Nesting exercises that recursion twice. It also pins the `spaceAfter` rule: a predicate followed by a GroupEnd emits NO trailing space, so the inner group closes as `... = ?))` with no gap, while a predicate followed by AND does emit one. Expected shape: `WHERE a AND (b OR (c AND d))`, params a,b,c,d in that order.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[a] = @p0 AND ([u].[b] = @p1 OR ([u].[c] = @p2 AND [u].[d] = @p3));', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint, @p3 tinyint', @p0 = 1, @p1 = 2, @p2 = 3, @p3 = 4;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[a] = 1 AND ([u].[b] = 2 OR ([u].[c] = 3 AND [u].[d] = 4));

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`a` = ? AND (`u`.`b` = ? OR (`u`.`c` = ? AND `u`.`d` = ?));

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE `u`.`a` = 1 AND (`u`.`b` = 2 OR (`u`.`c` = 3 AND `u`.`d` = 4));

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = $1 AND ("u"."b" = $2 OR ("u"."c" = $3 AND "u"."d" = $4));

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = 1 AND ("u"."b" = 2 OR ("u"."c" = 3 AND "u"."d" = 4));

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = ? AND ("u"."b" = ? OR ("u"."c" = ? AND "u"."d" = ?));

sqlite.raw: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE "u"."a" = 1 AND ("u"."b" = 2 OR ("u"."c" = 3 AND "u"."d" = 4));

cases.name: whereNotExists ignores the table and column it is given
cases.note: The signature is `whereNotExistsWithBuilder(table, column, builder)` and the parser uses NEITHER: the NotExists branch of defaultWhere emits `NOT EXISTS (` + subquery + `)` and never touches tableNameOrAlias/columnName. The deliberately absurd arguments here must not appear anywhere in the output on any dialect. A Dart port that helpfully renders `"IGNORED"."ALSO_IGNORED" NOT EXISTS (...)`, or that validates the arguments, diverges. The correlated predicate inside the subquery is the only thing tying it to the outer row.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND [o].[total] > @p0);', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 9;

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND [o].[total] > 9);

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `users` AS `u` WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND `o`.`total` > 9);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND "o"."total" > $1);

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."users" AS "u" WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND "o"."total" > 9);

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "users" AS "u" WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT * FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND "o"."total" > ?);

cases.name: a cte in front of an update binds before the set and where values
cases.note: to-sql emits the WITH clause BEFORE it branches on queryType, so a CTE attached to an UPDATE renders `WITH "stale" AS (SELECT ...) UPDATE ...` — and its values occupy the FIRST parameter slots, ahead of the SET value and then the WHERE value. Nothing in the authored corpus combines a CTE with a write statement; a port that builds writes through a separate code path drops the WITH entirely (silently updating everything) or binds the CTE value last. On MSSQL this also stacks with the aliased-UPDATE rewrite: `UPDATE [u] SET ... FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE ...`, with the FROM appended after the SET list.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [stale] AS (SELECT [s].[id] FROM [dbo].[sessions] AS [s] WHERE [s].[seen_at] < @p0) UPDATE [u] SET [status] = @p1 FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] > @p2;', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max), @p2 tinyint', @p0 = '2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z', @p1 = N'expired', @p2 = 10;

mssql.raw: WITH [stale] AS (SELECT [s].[id] FROM [dbo].[sessions] AS [s] WHERE [s].[seen_at] < 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z) UPDATE [u] SET [status] = expired FROM [dbo].[users] AS [u] WHERE [u].[id] > 10;

prepared.sql: WITH `stale` AS (SELECT `s`.`id` FROM `sessions` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`seen_at` < ?) UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `status` = ? WHERE `u`.`id` > ?;

mysql.raw: WITH `stale` AS (SELECT `s`.`id` FROM `sessions` AS `s` WHERE `s`.`seen_at` < 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z) UPDATE `users` AS `u` SET `status` = expired WHERE `u`.`id` > 10;

prepared.sql: WITH "stale" AS (SELECT "s"."id" FROM "public"."sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."seen_at" < $1) UPDATE "public"."users" AS "u" SET "status" = $2 WHERE "u"."id" > $3;

postgres.raw: WITH "stale" AS (SELECT "s"."id" FROM "public"."sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."seen_at" < 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z) UPDATE "public"."users" AS "u" SET "status" = expired WHERE "u"."id" > 10;

prepared.sql: WITH "stale" AS (SELECT "s"."id" FROM "sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."seen_at" < ?) UPDATE "users" AS "u" SET "status" = ? WHERE "u"."id" > ?;

sqlite.raw: WITH "stale" AS (SELECT "s"."id" FROM "sessions" AS "s" WHERE "s"."seen_at" < 2024-01-01T00:00:00.000Z) UPDATE "users" AS "u" SET "status" = expired WHERE "u"."id" > 10;

cases.name: update with an owner on mysql throws; other dialects render the join and still discard order by and limit on update
cases.note: Two behaviours in one statement. (1) MySQL rejects `updateTableWithOwner` with `Update: MySQL does not support table owners` — thrown before the join is even considered. (2) On dialects that accept the owner, the join IS now rendered (MSSQL: `FROM ... JOIN ... ON`; Postgres: `FROM <table> WHERE <translated ON predicate> AND <where>`; SQLite: throws, since it has no multi-table UPDATE syntax at all). `orderByColumn` and `limit` on this builder still produce nothing — no error, no SQL — since ORDER BY/LIMIT on UPDATE remain out of scope.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [o] SET [total] = @p0 FROM [sales].[orders] AS [o] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [c].[tier] = @p1 WHERE [o].[id] = @p2;', N'@p0 float, @p1 tinyint, @p2 tinyint', @p0 = 9.5, @p1 = 7, @p2 = 1;

mssql.raw: UPDATE [o] SET [total] = 9.5 FROM [sales].[orders] AS [o] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [c].[tier] = 7 WHERE [o].[id] = 1;

mysql.throws: Update: MySQL does not support table owners

sqlite.throws: Update: SQLite does not support joins in UPDATE/DELETE; rewrite the join as a correlated subquery

cases.name: multi builder restarts placeholder numbering per statement and concatenates without a separator
cases.note: parseMulti loops `sql += parse(state, config)` — each statement is prepared INDEPENDENTLY, so Postgres numbers both statements from $1 (the second statement's values are NOT $3/$4) and MSSQL emits two complete `SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql ...` batches. There is NO separator between statements: each ends with ';' and the next begins immediately, so the golden contains `...;INSERT` with no space, while the transaction prologue DOES add one (`BEGIN; `). Note also that parseMulti closes with `COMMIT;` while parseMultiRaw closes with `COMMIT; ` — a trailing space in raw only. A port that joins statements with '\n' or ' ', or that threads one placeholder counter across the batch, fails.

prepared.sql: BEGIN TRANSACTION; SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [age]) VALUES (@p0, @p1);', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 tinyint', @p0 = N'Ada', @p1 = 36;SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [dbo].[users] SET [active] = @p0 WHERE [users].[name] = @p1;', N'@p0 bit, @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = 1, @p1 = N'Ada';COMMIT TRANSACTION;

mssql.raw: BEGIN TRANSACTION; INSERT INTO [dbo].[users] ([name], [age]) VALUES (Ada, 36);UPDATE [dbo].[users] SET [active] = true WHERE [users].[name] = Ada;COMMIT TRANSACTION;

prepared.sql: START TRANSACTION; INSERT INTO `users` (`name`, `age`) VALUES (?, ?);UPDATE `users` SET `active` = ? WHERE `users`.`name` = ?;COMMIT;

mysql.raw: START TRANSACTION; INSERT INTO `users` (`name`, `age`) VALUES (Ada, 36);UPDATE `users` SET `active` = true WHERE `users`.`name` = Ada;COMMIT;

prepared.sql: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("name", "age") VALUES ($1, $2);UPDATE "public"."users" SET "active" = $1 WHERE "users"."name" = $2;COMMIT;

postgres.raw: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("name", "age") VALUES (Ada, 36);UPDATE "public"."users" SET "active" = true WHERE "users"."name" = Ada;COMMIT;

prepared.sql: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "users" ("name", "age") VALUES (?, ?);UPDATE "users" SET "active" = ? WHERE "users"."name" = ?;COMMIT;

sqlite.raw: BEGIN; INSERT INTO "users" ("name", "age") VALUES (Ada, 36);UPDATE "users" SET "active" = true WHERE "users"."name" = Ada;COMMIT;

cases.note: Upsert composes with RETURNING/OUTPUT: PG/SQLite append RETURNING after ON CONFLICT. MySQL's INSERT IGNORE succeeds, then the trailing RETURNING throws (MySQL has no such clause). cases.name: cte with an explicit column list
cases.note: Milestone 4: WITH name (col1, col2) AS (...).

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [recent] ([id], [total]) AS (SELECT [o].[id], [o].[total] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o]) SELECT * FROM [dbo].[recent] AS [r];', N'';

mssql.raw: WITH [recent] ([id], [total]) AS (SELECT [o].[id], [o].[total] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o]) SELECT * FROM [dbo].[recent] AS [r];

mysql.raw: WITH `recent` (`id`, `total`) AS (SELECT `o`.`id`, `o`.`total` FROM `orders` AS `o`) SELECT * FROM `recent` AS `r`;

cases.name: recursive cte with an explicit column list
cases.note: Milestone 4: WITH RECURSIVE name (col1, col2) AS (...) — MSSQL uses bare WITH (no RECURSIVE keyword) but keeps the column list.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'WITH [org_chart] ([id], [manager_id]) AS (SELECT [e].[id], [e].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e] WHERE [e].[id] = @p0 UNION ALL SELECT [e2].[id], [e2].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e2] INNER JOIN [dbo].[org_chart] AS [oc] ON [e2].[manager_id] = [oc].[id]) SELECT * FROM [dbo].[org_chart] AS [oc];', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: WITH [org_chart] ([id], [manager_id]) AS (SELECT [e].[id], [e].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e] WHERE [e].[id] = 1 UNION ALL SELECT [e2].[id], [e2].[manager_id] FROM [dbo].[employees] AS [e2] INNER JOIN [dbo].[org_chart] AS [oc] ON [e2].[manager_id] = [oc].[id]) SELECT * FROM [dbo].[org_chart] AS [oc];

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE `org_chart` (`id`, `manager_id`) AS (SELECT `e`.`id`, `e`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`id` = ? UNION ALL SELECT `e2`.`id`, `e2`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e2` INNER JOIN `org_chart` AS `oc` ON `e2`.`manager_id` = `oc`.`id`) SELECT * FROM `org_chart` AS `oc`;

mysql.raw: WITH RECURSIVE `org_chart` (`id`, `manager_id`) AS (SELECT `e`.`id`, `e`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e` WHERE `e`.`id` = 1 UNION ALL SELECT `e2`.`id`, `e2`.`manager_id` FROM `employees` AS `e2` INNER JOIN `org_chart` AS `oc` ON `e2`.`manager_id` = `oc`.`id`) SELECT * FROM `org_chart` AS `oc`;

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE "org_chart" ("id", "manager_id") AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = $1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e2" INNER JOIN "public"."org_chart" AS "oc" ON "e2"."manager_id" = "oc"."id") SELECT * FROM "public"."org_chart" AS "oc";

postgres.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "org_chart" ("id", "manager_id") AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = 1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "public"."employees" AS "e2" INNER JOIN "public"."org_chart" AS "oc" ON "e2"."manager_id" = "oc"."id") SELECT * FROM "public"."org_chart" AS "oc";

prepared.sql: WITH RECURSIVE "org_chart" ("id", "manager_id") AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = ? UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e2" INNER JOIN "org_chart" AS "oc" ON "e2"."manager_id" = "oc"."id") SELECT * FROM "org_chart" AS "oc";

sqlite.raw: WITH RECURSIVE "org_chart" ("id", "manager_id") AS (SELECT "e"."id", "e"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e" WHERE "e"."id" = 1 UNION ALL SELECT "e2"."id", "e2"."manager_id" FROM "employees" AS "e2" INNER JOIN "org_chart" AS "oc" ON "e2"."manager_id" = "oc"."id") SELECT * FROM "org_chart" AS "oc";

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'INSERT INTO [dbo].[archived_orders] ([id], [total]) SELECT [o].[id], [o].[total] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[archived] = @p0;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: INSERT INTO [dbo].[archived_orders] ([id], [total]) SELECT [o].[id], [o].[total] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] WHERE [o].[archived] = true;

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO `archived_orders` (`id`, `total`) SELECT `o`.`id`, `o`.`total` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`archived` = ?;

mysql.raw: INSERT INTO `archived_orders` (`id`, `total`) SELECT `o`.`id`, `o`.`total` FROM `orders` AS `o` WHERE `o`.`archived` = true;

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO "public"."archived_orders" ("id", "total") SELECT "o"."id", "o"."total" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."archived" = $1;

postgres.raw: INSERT INTO "public"."archived_orders" ("id", "total") SELECT "o"."id", "o"."total" FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."archived" = true;

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO "archived_orders" ("id", "total") SELECT "o"."id", "o"."total" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."archived" = ?;

sqlite.raw: INSERT INTO "archived_orders" ("id", "total") SELECT "o"."id", "o"."total" FROM "orders" AS "o" WHERE "o"."archived" = true;

cases.name: insert select combined with insertValues throws
cases.note: Milestone 4: INSERT cannot mix a SELECT source with a literal VALUES row — mutually exclusive, caught at parse time.

mssql.throws: Insert: INSERT cannot combine a SELECT source with VALUES rows

mysql.throws: Insert: INSERT cannot combine a SELECT source with VALUES rows

postgres.throws: Insert: INSERT cannot combine a SELECT source with VALUES rows

sqlite.throws: Insert: INSERT cannot combine a SELECT source with VALUES rows

cases.name: update with join on mysql renders multi-table update syntax
cases.note: Milestone 4: MySQL's multi-table UPDATE puts `JOIN ... ON` directly after the target, before SET.

prepared.sql: UPDATE `orders` AS `o` INNER JOIN `customers` AS `c` ON `o`.`customer_id` = `c`.`id` SET `total` = ? WHERE `c`.`banned` = ?;

mysql.raw: UPDATE `orders` AS `o` INNER JOIN `customers` AS `c` ON `o`.`customer_id` = `c`.`id` SET `total` = 0 WHERE `c`.`banned` = true;

cases.name: update with join on postgres translates the on condition into a where predicate
cases.note: Milestone 4: Postgres cannot JOIN the target row to a from_item with ON — that condition becomes a WHERE predicate, ANDed in front of the caller's own where().

prepared.sql: UPDATE "public"."orders" AS "o" SET "total" = $1 FROM "public"."customers" AS "c" WHERE "o"."customer_id" = "c"."id" AND "c"."banned" = $2;

postgres.raw: UPDATE "public"."orders" AS "o" SET "total" = 0 FROM "public"."customers" AS "c" WHERE "o"."customer_id" = "c"."id" AND "c"."banned" = true;

cases.name: update with join on mssql renders update from join syntax
cases.note: Milestone 4: T-SQL's UPDATE...FROM accepts full JOIN...ON syntax, so this reuses the same defaultJoin rendering as SELECT.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [o] SET [total] = @p0 FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [o].[customer_id] = [c].[id] WHERE [c].[banned] = @p1;', N'@p0 tinyint, @p1 bit', @p0 = 0, @p1 = 1;

mssql.raw: UPDATE [o] SET [total] = 0 FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [o].[customer_id] = [c].[id] WHERE [c].[banned] = true;

cases.name: update with join on sqlite throws
cases.note: Milestone 4: SQLite has no multi-table UPDATE/JOIN syntax at all, so a join-backed UPDATE is rejected outright rather than silently dropped.

sqlite.throws: Update: SQLite does not support joins in UPDATE/DELETE; rewrite the join as a correlated subquery

cases.name: update with join and no explicit alias on mssql still uses the from-join form
cases.note: Milestone 4: MSSQL needs UPDATE...FROM to attach a JOIN even when the caller never gave the target table an alias — the bare table name is reused as its own FROM reference.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'UPDATE [dbo].[orders] SET [total] = @p0 FROM [dbo].[orders] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [orders].[customer_id] = [c].[id];', N'@p0 tinyint', @p0 = 0;

mssql.raw: UPDATE [dbo].[orders] SET [total] = 0 FROM [dbo].[orders] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [orders].[customer_id] = [c].[id];

cases.name: update with a cross join on postgres has no where predicate contribution
cases.note: Milestone 4: a CROSS JOIN has no ON condition, so it contributes an unconditional extra FROM item and no extra WHERE predicate.

prepared.sql: UPDATE "public"."orders" AS "o" SET "flagged" = $1 FROM "public"."flags" AS "f";

postgres.raw: UPDATE "public"."orders" AS "o" SET "flagged" = true FROM "public"."flags" AS "f";

cases.name: update with a left join on postgres throws
cases.note: Milestone 4: Postgres UPDATE...FROM only supports INNER/CROSS joins here — translating a LEFT JOIN's ON condition into a WHERE predicate would silently change it into an INNER JOIN, so it is rejected instead.

postgres.throws: Update: Postgres UPDATE...FROM/DELETE...USING only supports INNER or CROSS joins — the ON condition is translated into a WHERE predicate, which cannot express OUTER JOIN semantics

cases.name: delete with join using on postgres
cases.note: Milestone 4: DELETE...USING mirrors UPDATE...FROM — a plain comma from_item list plus a translated WHERE predicate.

prepared.sql: DELETE FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" USING "public"."customers" AS "c" WHERE "o"."customer_id" = "c"."id" AND "c"."banned" = $1;

postgres.raw: DELETE FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" USING "public"."customers" AS "c" WHERE "o"."customer_id" = "c"."id" AND "c"."banned" = true;

cases.name: delete with join on mysql renders multi-table delete syntax
cases.note: Milestone 4: MySQL's multi-table DELETE names the target right after DELETE, then joins the rest in FROM.

prepared.sql: DELETE `o` FROM `orders` AS `o` INNER JOIN `customers` AS `c` ON `o`.`customer_id` = `c`.`id` WHERE `c`.`banned` = ?;

mysql.raw: DELETE `o` FROM `orders` AS `o` INNER JOIN `customers` AS `c` ON `o`.`customer_id` = `c`.`id` WHERE `c`.`banned` = true;

cases.name: delete with join on mssql renders delete from join syntax
cases.note: Milestone 4: T-SQL's DELETE...FROM accepts full JOIN...ON syntax the same way UPDATE...FROM does.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'DELETE [o] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [o].[customer_id] = [c].[id] WHERE [c].[banned] = @p0;', N'@p0 bit', @p0 = 1;

mssql.raw: DELETE [o] FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] INNER JOIN [dbo].[customers] AS [c] ON [o].[customer_id] = [c].[id] WHERE [c].[banned] = true;

cases.name: delete with join on sqlite throws
cases.note: Milestone 4: same rejection as a join-backed UPDATE — SQLite has no multi-table DELETE/JOIN syntax.

sqlite.throws: Delete: SQLite does not support joins in UPDATE/DELETE; rewrite the join as a correlated subquery

cases.name: join on with like operator
cases.note: Milestone 4: JoinOperator.Like in a column-to-column ON condition — `ON a.col LIKE b.pattern`.

cases.name: mssql merge upsert do update
cases.note: MSSQL upsert is MERGE; PG/SQLite ON CONFLICT; MySQL ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE.

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'MERGE INTO [dbo].[users] AS [target] USING (VALUES (@p0, @p1)) AS [source] ([email], [name]) ON [target].[email] = [source].[email] WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT ([email], [name]) VALUES ([source].[email], [source].[name]) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET [name] = [source].[name];', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'a@b.c', @p1 = N'Ada';

mssql.raw: MERGE INTO [dbo].[users] AS [target] USING (VALUES (a@b.c, Ada)) AS [source] ([email], [name]) ON [target].[email] = [source].[email] WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT ([email], [name]) VALUES ([source].[email], [source].[name]) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET [name] = [source].[name];

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO `users` (`email`, `name`) VALUES (?, ?) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE `name` = ?;

mysql.raw: INSERT INTO `users` (`email`, `name`) VALUES (a@b.c, Ada) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE `name` = Grace;

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("email", "name") VALUES ($1, $2) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO UPDATE SET "name" = $3;

postgres.raw: INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("email", "name") VALUES (a@b.c, Ada) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO UPDATE SET "name" = Grace;

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO "users" ("email", "name") VALUES (?, ?) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO UPDATE SET "name" = ?;

sqlite.raw: INSERT INTO "users" ("email", "name") VALUES (a@b.c, Ada) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO UPDATE SET "name" = Grace;

cases.name: mssql merge upsert do nothing

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'MERGE INTO [dbo].[users] AS [target] USING (VALUES (@p0, @p1)) AS [source] ([email], [name]) ON [target].[email] = [source].[email] WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT ([email], [name]) VALUES ([source].[email], [source].[name]);', N'@p0 nvarchar(max), @p1 nvarchar(max)', @p0 = N'a@b.c', @p1 = N'Ada';

mssql.raw: MERGE INTO [dbo].[users] AS [target] USING (VALUES (a@b.c, Ada)) AS [source] ([email], [name]) ON [target].[email] = [source].[email] WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT ([email], [name]) VALUES ([source].[email], [source].[name]);

prepared.sql: INSERT IGNORE INTO `users` (`email`, `name`) VALUES (?, ?);

mysql.raw: INSERT IGNORE INTO `users` (`email`, `name`) VALUES (a@b.c, Ada);

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("email", "name") VALUES ($1, $2) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO NOTHING;

postgres.raw: INSERT INTO "public"."users" ("email", "name") VALUES (a@b.c, Ada) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO NOTHING;

prepared.sql: INSERT INTO "users" ("email", "name") VALUES (?, ?) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO NOTHING;

sqlite.raw: INSERT INTO "users" ("email", "name") VALUES (a@b.c, Ada) ON CONFLICT ("email") DO NOTHING;

cases.name: cross apply postgres lateral

prepared.sql: SET NOCOUNT ON; exec sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] CROSS APPLY (SELECT [li].[sku] FROM [dbo].[line_items] AS [li]) AS [x];', N'';

mssql.raw: SELECT * FROM [dbo].[orders] AS [o] CROSS APPLY (SELECT [li].[sku] FROM [dbo].[line_items] AS [li]) AS [x];

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM `orders` AS `o` CROSS JOIN LATERAL (SELECT `li`.`sku` FROM `line_items` AS `li`) AS `x`;

mysql.raw: SELECT * FROM `orders` AS `o` CROSS JOIN LATERAL (SELECT `li`.`sku` FROM `line_items` AS `li`) AS `x`;

prepared.sql: SELECT * FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" CROSS JOIN LATERAL (SELECT "li"."sku" FROM "public"."line_items" AS "li") AS "x";

postgres.raw: SELECT * FROM "public"."orders" AS "o" CROSS JOIN LATERAL (SELECT "li"."sku" FROM "public"."line_items" AS "li") AS "x";

sqlite.throws: Join: SQLite does not support CROSS APPLY/LATERAL joins
